title: Update Pydantic to v0.25
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Pydantic structural subtyping can't differentiate between two semantically different but structurally identical types. Hence, in the current Pydantic v0.25 release the `const` keyword was introduced to help with that, [Pydantic issue link](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/434). Unfortunately, at the moment FastAPI works with versions less than 0.23, `fastapi (0.20.0) depends on pydantic (>=0.17,<=0.23.0)`  

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I would like to update to pydantic v0.25
author: kolygri

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Done! Implemented by @euri10 in PR #225.

Just released (with some other changes) in FastAPI version `0.21.0`. :tada: :rocket: :cake:

title: Starlette path variable convertors not recognized as path variables
body:
**Describe the bug**
A clear and concise description of what the bug is.

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a file with the following code

```py
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

@app.get('/myblog/posts/{id}/{slug:path}')
async def view_post(request: Request, id:int, slug: str):
    return dict(request.path_params)
```

2. Open the browser and try accessing something like `http://127.0.0.1:8000/myblog/posts/42/my_article_is_very_insightful/this_could_be_anything/whatever`
3. See error.

```json
{"detail":[{"loc":["query","slug"],"msg":"field required","type":"value_error.missing"}]}
```

A few things to notice:
* FastAPI throws a validation error about a missing *__query__* parameter.
* Making the signature `view_post(request: Request, id:int):` (no `slug:str` parameter) causes the error to go away

```json
{"id":"42","slug":"my_article_is_very_insightful/this_could_be_anything/whatever"}
```

* The returned `request.query_params` object above (which I imagine comes from Starlette because `id` appears to be a string) still contains the value for `slug`, meaning it was recognized at some point but FastAPI didn't catch it.
* Likewise, making the route URL `'/myblog/posts/{id}/{slug}'` (no path converter) fixes the issue as long as the slug does not contain any slashes

**Expected behavior**
In the example provided above, the route should work the exact same way whether or not `slug:str` is in the function signature.

**Environment:**
 - OS: Windows
 - FastAPI Version: 0.20.0
 - Python version: Python 3.6.5

----

While most Starlette convertors are unlikely to be of much use to FastAPI applications (because of the type validation already being performed by FastAPI) ((as well as being undocumented)), the lack of a path wildcard in FastAPI means there still remains this last use case for them. I wouldn't personally mind if this issuewas closed in favor of a different implementation of path wildcards, although the contents of `request.path_params` raise some interesting questions regarding the way in which FastAPI currently performs route parameter extraction and why there appears to be a mismatch between the request object and that FastAPI sees.
author: sm-Fifteen

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
Does that help ?
https://github.com/euri10/fastapi/blob/slugbug/tests/test_slug.py

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
I think the main conflict we will find here is that there's no way to declare that in OpenAPI: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#pathTemplating

I think that FastAPI's implementation of routing is there from a pre-convertors version in Starlette.

We might think about a workaround, or having custom handling for convertors, although I'm not sure how would we represent that in OpenAPI. For example, there's no way to pass an actual path with Swagger UI. It converts `a/b/c` to `a%2Fb%2Fc`.

A current workaround is to not use FastAPI's validation and use Starlette's normal `@app.route('/myblog/posts/{id}/{slug:path}')`. But in that case you would have to handle validation, etc, by hand.

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body:
Ah, right, FastAPI is primarily about OpenAPI, I probably shouldn't try doing something like this in the first place (since it's more of a HTML page routing thing and that's what I was intending to use this for) and instead use Starlette directly.

Actually, that should probably be mentionned in the templating part of the doc, since there are fairly high chances that the people looking at this part are intending to use it for more traditional HTML responses.

EDIT: To be clear, I mean that the doc should mention that FastAPI should probably be used strictly for API purposes, and that if you want an API in conjunction with a web page, a Starlette root application with FastAPI mounted as an Endpoint would probably be preferable. FastAPI probably shouldn't try fixing this specofic "bug", because it would go against its goal of being a pythonic way of generating OpenAPI servers and definitions.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
@sm-Fifteen it was just solved by @euri10 in OAI/OpenAPI-Specification#234. It is released in FastAPI `0.22.0`.

It works with `a/b/c` and `a%2Fb%2Fc`, so, it ends up not being a problem with OpenAPI (even if it's a bit of kind of cheating).

To clarify a bit, FastAPI is made around APIs, and optimized for them, but still, the intention is to support most of the use cases. And the reason of being based in Starlette is to be able to inherit the awesome Starlette features.

So, the idea is that you should be able to do with FastAPI everything you can do with Starlette. And if you build APIs, you get extra benefits.

comments.author: euri10
comments.body: Soon custom converters available : https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/458

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body:
Looking into the OpenAPI spec further, it would appear that the correct way to do something like this according to both [OpenAPI 3.0](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/serialization/#uri-templates) and [RFC 6570](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6570#section-3.2.3) should actually be something closer to `/myblog/posts/{id}/{+slug}`, which would be translated in the path parameters as `allowReserved: true` (that spec is full of surprises!).

Should we be using this instead of [Flask-style path variable convertors](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/1.0/quickstart/#variable-rules)?

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
That's a very interesting finding @sm-Fifteen .

So, OpenAPI defines a way to `allowReserved: true`: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md#fixed-fields-10 but it only applies to parameters in `query`. Not in `path`.

The [example in Swagger you referenced](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/serialization/#uri-templates) says what are the convertor equivalences, but not where they are allowed.

Still, even though Swagger started all this, the spec lives now at: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/versions/3.0.2.md. So, the examples and docs at Swagger are nice and help, but in case of doubts (as this one), the "single source of truth" is the spec.

I see the example you found is cited in this issue: https://github.com/OAI/learn.openapis.org/issues/100 and they discuss a bit more about styles, although not specifically about path parameters with reserved characters.

For reserved characters in path parameters (exactly what we are discussing here), there's this issue, and they discuss specifically the use of `/myblog/posts/{id}/{+slug}`: https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/issues/1840:

> It is specifically disallowed on path parameters [...]

---

## In Summary

Allowing/handling `/myblog/posts/{id}/{+slug}` would not be spec-compliant, apart from the extra effort to parse it, etc... :sweat_smile: 

I think for now we should stick to the current solution, with the `:path` Starlette convertor, only for this specific case.

For the rest of the cases, we can use the normal parameter declarations with standard Python types.

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body:
Oh, yeah, I get that it's not how you'd be writing your routes in an OpenAPI definition. My point is that the various route and query parameter options are based off of RFC 6570, meaning that a lot of complex route formats that OpenAPI can accept can be expressed in one URL template (and not specifically just `allowReserved: true`/`{+path}`, there could be interesting uses for `style: matrix`/`{;list}` or `style: label`/`{.list}`, not to mention the possibility of adding the query parameters to the route directly to avoid the need for disambiguation between body and query parameters).

`{mypath:path}` isn't exactly a lot more OpenAPI-compliant than `{+mypath}`, I just figured that most starlette path convertors are (`{id:int}`, `{distance:float}`) already kind of at odds with the way FastAPI handles type conversion with `path` just being the odd one out, and since most (if not all) OpenAPI parameter formats have an RFC 6570 equivalent while FastAPI tries to put as much information as possible in its function signatures and route annotations that this could be a good way to move parameter properties there.

With that said, though, this might be getting off-topic from the original issue.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Hehe yep.

Supporting the other templating formats might be something that we can think about in the future. For now I think we can use the current ideas.

And yes, `{mypath:path}` is not OpenAPI-compliant either... But given that it's something not "officially" supported by OpenAPI, keeping the Starlette implementation should work for these use cases, at least for now.

As this is (more or less) solved now, should we close this issue then?

comments.author: sm-Fifteen
comments.body: Certainly, I'll probably just open a separate feature request for RFC 6570 templates at some point later.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Cool, thanks!

title: Configurable Swagger UI
body:
I want to be able to set configurations of Swagger UI, but I can't do it the right way as it is hard coded right now.
[Reference to Swagger UI configuration](https://swagger.io/docs/open-source-tools/swagger-ui/usage/configuration/)

**Describe the solution you'd like**
I would love to be able to set configurations through FastAPI object.

The normal Swagger UI configurations are easy to implement. However there are also some methods like _initOAuth_ which requests some additional checks in code.

My suggesetion would be to replace the current parameter _openapi_url_ of het method _fastapi/openapi/docs.py->get_swagger_ui_html_ with an Pydantic object with the defaults set with the current values for _openapi_url_.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
Alternative solution is to set docs_url to None and create a get method with a HTMLResponse as return value. The returned HTMLResponse is identical to _fastapi/openapi/docs.py->get_swagger_ui_html_ method, with some additional configurations and the _initOAuth_ method.

**Additional context**
**Why do I need this?** 
With a custom company Identity & Access Management with support for OAuth2 in place, we need some additional configuration in Swagger UI to get it to work. 
One of the items is already in an PR #198. (alternative for this solution is also a get method with HTMLResponse as a return value)

Some other settings we would like to set in Swagger UI are:

- oauth2RedirectUrl setting, this makes it possible to redirect to another page then Swaggers default _http://url/oauth2-redirect.html_.
- The method initOAuth [Reference to Swagger UI OAuth2 configuration](https://swagger.io/docs/open-source-tools/swagger-ui/usage/oauth2/) 

I hope it is clear, if you have any questions let me know.
author: zamiramir

title: make swagger_ui_html, redoc_html and openapi.json handled by async function?
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
I add a loop on all my router of my app to check if the handler are all coroutine function to get better performance, but I fount that swagger_ui_html, redoc_html and openapi.json are handled by normal function.

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/56ab106bbbf8054af437821c6683491ca7952c3b/fastapi/applications.py#L80-L86

**Describe the solution you'd like**

I'm wondering if its possible to handle these 3 router with async function, as its only a simple `getattr` operator or concat string.

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/56ab106bbbf8054af437821c6683491ca7952c3b/fastapi/applications.py#L78

```python
    def setup(self) -> None:
        if self.openapi_url:

            async def openapi_handler(req):
                return JSONResponse(self.openapi())

            self.add_route(
                self.openapi_url,
                openapi_handler,
                include_in_schema=False,
            )
```
**Describe alternatives you've considered**

Disable default handlers and I write async handlers by my self to replace inner handlers.

**Additional context**

In `Starlette`, if a handler is not coroutine function, it will be executed by `loop.run_in_executor` . So this could improve performance a litte bit.(In my test, about 2800 r/s to 3200r/s)

title: HTTPException detail type
body:
**Description**

I'm not sure if this is a bug since I'm not sure what the fix would be and it _seems_ to be covered in the docs, though not explicitly.

Per the docs you can pass anything JSON serializable to `HTTPException`'s `detail` parameter: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/

However, it's typed as `str`, presumably because it's inheriting Starlette's HTTPException, and I assume it does this to get some specific functionality from Starlette. So for now I have to just pass in my JSON serializable content and stick `# type: ignore` on the end.

I'm just wondering if there's a better way to do this, or if it's something that can be fixed.
author: knyghty

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the report.

This should be fixed and available since `0.27.1`, the current latest (just released) is `0.27.2`. :tada: :rocket:

comments.body: Thanks, it's fixed for me now.

title: Add support for Pydantic's dataclasses
body:
**Describe the question**
I am using a  pydantic.dataclasses.dataclass as a response_model of my fast api route.


```
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class Foo:
    bar: int

app = FastAPI()

@app.get('/foo', response_model=Foo)
def get_bar() -> Foo:
    return Foo(bar=1)
```

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create a file **app.py** with the above code.
2. Start the app: `uvicorn app:app`
3. Open the browser and go to **localhost:8000/foo**
4. See error: 
```
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error
response
  __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument '__initialised__' (type=type_error)
```

**Expected behavior**
No validation error and serialized json does not contain any keys `__initialised__`.


**Environment:**
 - OS: Linux
 - FastAPI Version 0.25.0
- Python version 3.7.3
author: sebastianschramm

comments.author: sebastianschramm
comments.body:
**Possible solution**

Add the following to jsonable_encoder in fastapi.encoders:

```
from dataclasses import is_dataclass, asdict

def jsonable_encoder(
    obj: Any,
    include: Set[str] = None,
    exclude: Set[str] = set(),
    by_alias: bool = True,
    skip_defaults: bool = False,
    include_none: bool = True,
    custom_encoder: dict = {},
    sqlalchemy_safe: bool = True,
) -> Any:
...
    if is_dataclass(obj):
        return asdict(obj)
```

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Thank you. Pydantic's dataclasses are not supported yet. They probably will be at some point.

👍 for this.  I have a backend code base that heavily relies on pydantic dataclasses.

Just a thought, the way pydantic dataclasses are handled is by putting the `BaseModel` subclass at `cls.__pydantic_model__`, e.g.:

```python
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass
from pydantic import BaseModel

@dataclass
class Foobar:
    pass

assert issubclass(Foobar.__pydantic_model__, BaseModel)
```

Could this be solved with a simple:
```python
if isdataclass(cls) and hasattr(cls, "__pydantic_model__"):
    cls = cls.__pydantic_model__
```
Somewhere?

Hi,

Error: 
```
pydantic.error_wrappers.ValidationError: 1 validation error for XYZResponse
response
  value is not a valid dict (type=type_error.dict)
```

Code:
```python
@dataclass
class XYZResponse:

    Y: SomeOtherDataclass

comments.author: jomido
comments.body:
Is it more likely that tools such as `mypy` and `pyright` will opt to do type checking on built-in objects, such as Python's standard `dataclasses`, vs library-supplied objects, like `FastAPI`'s `BaseModel` and `pydantic.dataclasses`?

I think it might. In addition, `TypedDict` (another built-in come Python 3.8), is already being checked (to varying degrees of success) by the aforementioned type checkers.

comments.author: dmontagu
dmontagu
samuelcolvin
comments.body: I would guess the best approach would be a check in fastapi that `orm_mode` is enabled, perhaps powered by some pydantic tool methods like `get_model` or `get_config` that work on both models and dataclasses?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
That's a good idea, I think we should add that check to FastAPI in the near future. In particular, raising an error if a `response_model` is specified that doesn't have `orm_mode = True`. Or maybe just if the annotated return type differs from the response_model (so that `orm_mode` might be explicitly relevant).

Alternatively, perhaps we should automatically enable `orm_mode` as necessary, possibly by subclassing the provided `response_model` with an update to the `Config`. That way we don't introduce errors into any existing code.

comments.author: Imaclean74
comments.body: agreed with this. I've just hit the same issue - and the logged error ("value is not a valid dict (type=type_error.dict)") is less than informative. The orm_mode fix does work as expected though - so that's good.

title: Generic typehints causing mypy errors
body:
**Describe the bug**
The following code results in a mypy error
```python
from typing import List
from fastapi import FastAPI, params

def admin_dependencies() -> List[params.Security]:
    return []

app = FastAPI()

@app.get("/", dependencies=admin_dependencies())
def admin_endpoint():
    pass
```
The mypy error is
```
error: Argument "dependencies" to "get" of "FastAPI" has incompatible
    type "List[Security]"; expected "Optional[List[Depends]]"
```
This error is occurring because `List` is invariant. However, if I understand correctly, in this case `dependencies` can be safely type hinted as `Sequence`, which is covariant (and would fix the mypy error). This specific case (for `dependencies`) occurs in a few places that could probably be replaced.

This is probably also the case for the `routes` argument to `FastAPI`.
author: dmontagu

comments.body:

This is handled in #338 and will be included in the next release :rocket:

title: OpenAPI fails when forcing query params to be body and multiple params in each
body:
**Describe the bug**

The OpenAPI spec for my app breaks with the following error (shown in my web browser).

```
Fetch error
Internal Server Error /openapi.json
```

This only happens when I have endpoints defined in two separate files, each of which force all their arguments to be body parameters via annotating each parameter with `Body(...)` to force them to be body parameters, as opposed to query parameters.

Check this out!

- If the endpoints are defined in the same file, then the problem does not happen.
- If each endpoint only has a single parameter, the problem does not happen.
- If only one endpoint has multiple parameters, then the problem does not happen.

It only happens when both endpoints have *multiple* parameters annotated with `Body(...)`.


**To Reproduce**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Create a directory with the following structure.

```
.
├── foo
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── bar.py
│   └── baz.py
└── main.py
```

main.py

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI

import foo.bar
import foo.baz

app = FastAPI()

app.get('/v1/compute')(foo.bar.compute)
app.get('/v2/compute')(foo.baz.compute)
```

foo/bar.py

```python
from fastapi import Body

def compute(
        a: int = Body(...),
        b: str = Body(...),
):
    return a + b 
```

foo/baz.py (identical to foo/bar.py)

```python
from fastapi import Body

def compute(
        a: int = Body(...),
        b: str = Body(...),
):
    return a + b
```

2. Run with `uvicorn main:app --reload`
3. Visit `http://localhost:8000/docs`

**Expected behavior**

The OpenAPI page shows without error.

**Screenshots**

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2068912/58768563-3e448080-8552-11e9-92b1-cfc1e8040d68.png)

Here's the exception my app produces when I try to visit `http://localhost:8000`.

```

WARNING:root:email-validator not installed, email fields will be treated as str.

  File "/Users/edwardbanner/.anaconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uvicorn/protocols/http/httptools_impl.py", line 368, in run_asgi
    result = await app(self.scope, self.receive, self.send)
  File "/Users/edwardbanner/.anaconda/lib/python3.6/site-packages/uvicorn/middleware/asgi2.py", line 7, in __call__

comments.author: DannyVarod
Kludex
comments.body:
I can't reproduce your issue @DannyVarod 

Run as `pytest <filename>`:
```python
from typing import Dict, Any

import pytest
from fastapi import FastAPI, Body
from fastapi.testclient import TestClient


app = FastAPI()

@app.post('/op1/{param1}')
async def op1(param1: int, my_dict: Dict[Any, Any]):
    return my_dict

@app.post('/op2/{param1}')
async def op2(param1: int, my_dict: Dict[Any, Any] = Body(...)):
   return my_dict

@app.post('/op3/{param1}')
async def op3(param1: int, my_dict: Dict[Any, Any] = Body(default=dict(), embed=False)):
    return my_dict

client = TestClient(app)

@pytest.mark.parametrize("endpoint", ["op1", "op2", "op3"])
def test_everything(endpoint: str):
    param1 = 1
    my_dict = {"potato": "potato"}

    res = client.post(f"/{endpoint}/{param1}", json=my_dict)
    assert res.json() == my_dict
```

comments.author: DannyVarod
comments.body: @Kludex, how comes you used `json=my_dict` and not `data=my_dict` or `json=json.dumps(my_dict)`?

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
@DannyVarod the test client is based on Requests, the `json` parameter take pure Python data types and converts them to serialized JSON. The `data` parameter is for form data. `json=json.dumps(my_dict)` would pass a single string to `json`, then Requests would send that as a JSON string type, not as a JSON object.

> Sorry for the long delay! 🙈  I wanted to personally address each issue/PR and they piled up through time, but now I'm checking each one in order.

comments.author: DannyVarod
comments.body: @tiangolo I have since figured this out. The names are misleading though,m because JSON is a form of serializing data into a string - not an object. I would expect data to be for original data and json for **serialized** data (into JSON), incase someone wants to use a custom serializer.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body: Yep, it's an old design decision by Requests 🤷😅

title: Access request body when reusing FastAPI's exception handlers
body:
**Description**

First let me say: FastAPI is exceptionally useful to me and my team. You are doing great work here! Thank you very much.

Now to my question: **How can I access the request body when writing my own exception handlers?**

I understand that I get the request object of starlette as an argument to my own exception handler. However to access its body the coroutine request.body() must be executed (or is there any other way?). When I try to call this coroutine like in this example:

```
@app.exception_handler(RequestValidationError)
async def validation_exception_handler(request, exc):
    request_body = await request.body()
    logger.info(
        "Request validation failed:\n" + str(exc) + "\nRequest:\n" + str(request_body)
    )
    return await request_validation_exception_handler(request, exc)
```
the application hangs indefinitely / the event loop somehow freezes.

I tried to achieve the same using FastAPI's middleware decorator but that leads to the same problem.

Is it possible to to access the request body in my own exception handler? If yes, what is the recommended way?

title: Awaiting request body in middleware blocks the application
body:
**Describe the bug**
Description in the title

**To Reproduce**
Minimal code:
```
from typing import Mapping

from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

app = FastAPI()

@app.middleware("http")
async def func(request: Request, call_next):
    print(await request.json())
    return await call_next(request)

@app.post("/")
def read_root(arg: Mapping[str, str]):
    return {"Hello": "World"}
```
Run the application with ```uvicorn <file>:app```
Test the bug with ```curl localhost:8000 -d '{"status":"ok"}'```

**Expected behavior**
The body of the request is printed, but the curl command stay pending for ever. If it is interrupted (Ctrl-C), the application then print ```ERROR: Error getting request body:```

**Environment:**
 - OS: macOS
 - fastapi 0.33.0
 - python 3.7.3
 - (tested on Ubuntu too with Python 3.7.0 and 3.7.4)

**Additional context**
 - When the route function has no body argument (```def read_root():```), there is no problem : the body is printed and the response send.
 - Thinking the issue was maybe coming from Starlette, I tested the following code, which works without issue. The bug seems thus to come from fastapi
```
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

app = Starlette()


@app.middleware("http")
async def func(request: Request, call_next):
    print(await request.json())
    return await call_next(request)


@app.route('/', methods=["POST"])
def homepage(request):
    return JSONResponse({"Hello": "World"})
```
author: wyfo

comments.author: euri10
wyfo
comments.body:
> works fine for me copy-pasting the exact same thing, only had to comment ` print(await request.json())` which crashed the app

That's exactly the point, the print statement, or more exactly the ``àwait``` inside blocks the application. The question is why ?
And the other question, more important for me is : how can I log my request payload if i'm not able to await it in middleware ?

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
I think the middleware is unnecessary, should you want to get your payload just do:
```
@app.post("/bug")
async def read_root(request: Request, arg: Mapping[str, str]):
    print(await request.json())
    return {"Hello": "World"}
```

comments.author: wyfo
comments.body:
> I think the middleware is unnecessary, should you want to get your payload just do:

But i don't want to just get the payload of one request, i want to log the payloads of EVERY endpoints of my application. I don't want for that to add code in every endpoint, that's the purpose of a middleware.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@wyfo It appears this is sort of a Starlette problem -- if you try to access `request.json()` both inside of and outside of a middleware, you'll run into the same problem you are hitting with fastapi. This has to do with how the json is "cached" inside the starlette `Request` -- it isn't transferred to the next called asgi app.

You can reproduce the issue in the pure starlette example if you try to print the json contents inside your starlette endpoint:

* change `def homepage(request):` it to use `async def`
* add a line containing `print(await request.json())`

FastAPI grabs the request.json() as part of it's request handling, which is why you run into the issue even without explicitly trying to if using FastAPI.

In order for this to be handled properly, I think it would require fixes in starlette; I feel like I've seen this discussed in starlette issues, but I'm not sure. I'll reference it if I find it. Even if you weren't using FastAPI, you'd face this issue if you didn't do something funny with to store the json after the first time you read it.

-------

On the other hand, I think there is a neat workaround for this use case (that actually requires FastAPI), since you don't need your middleware to modify anything before the next handler receives it:

```python
from typing import Mapping

from starlette.requests import Request

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter, Depends

app = FastAPI()
api_router = APIRouter()

@api_router.post("/")
def read_root(arg: Mapping[str, str]):
    return {"Hello": "World"}

async def log_json(request: Request):
    print(await request.json())

# the trick here is including log_json in the dependencies:
app.include_router(api_router, dependencies=[Depends(log_json)])
```
This requires you to add all endpoints to `api_router` rather than `app`, but ensures that `log_json` will be called for every endpoint added to it (functioning very similarly to a middleware, given that all endpoints pass through `api_router`).

This works because no new `Request` instance will be created as part of the asgi request handling process, so the json read off by FastAPI's processing will still be cached on the request when the `log_json` function is called.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@wyfo For what it's worth, it looks like consuming the body inside of middleware is somewhat broadly discouraged -- https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495#issuecomment-494008175 (by the creator of starlette):

> Consuming request data in middleware is problematic.
> Not just to Starlette, but generally, everywhere.
> 
> On the whole you should avoid doing so if at all possible.

So the "workaround" I posted above may actually be a better way to handle this than middleware anyway.

comments.author: wyfo
comments.body:
@dmontagu
Thank you a lot ! For your workaround and for retrieving the starlette issue. I don't really understand the point of @tomchristie about that, but it seems I have to continue the discussion in the dedicated place.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for your help @euri10 and @dmontagu.

Agreed with all of @dmontagu's explanation and suggestion.

Thanks @wyfo for reporting back and closing the issue.

>  async def set_body(request: Request, body: bytes):
>      async def receive() -> Message:
>          return {"type": "http.request", "body": body}
>      request._receive = receive
>  
>  async def get_body(request: Request) -> bytes:
>      body = await request.body()
>      await set_body(request, body)
>      return body
>  
>  @app.middleware("http")
>  async def app_entry(request: Request, call_next):
>      
>      await set_body(request, request.body())
>  
>      print(await get_body(request))
>  
>      response = await call_next(request)
>      return response
> ```

```python
await set_body(request, request.body()) -> await set_body(request, await request.body())
```
Looks like await should be added.

comments.author: king-peanut
comments.body:
For anyone who is looking for class-based middleware:
```python
class LoggingMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):

    def __init__(self, app):
        super().__init__(app)

    async def set_body(self, request: Request):
        receive_ = await request._receive()

        async def receive() -> Message:
            return receive_

        request._receive = receive

    async def dispatch(self, request, call_next):
        await self.set_body(request)
        body = await request.body()
        json_body = await request.json()

        print(body)
        print(json_body)

        response = await call_next(request)

        return response
```
Many thanks for @liukelin

comments.author: XaviTorello
comments.body:
This workaround works as expected many thanks @king-peanut.

The unique condition is that this should be placed as the last middleware (the first to be loaded into the app, take care about the reverse ordering), if not it will freeze.

comments.author: Nick1994209
comments.body:
If you're using StreamingResponse in your response.

```python
    receive_ = await request._receive()

    async def receive() -> Message:
        return receive_

    request._receive = receive
```

Does not work =(

But you can write

```python
async def get_request_body(request: Request) -> bytes:
    body = await request.body()

    request._receive = ReceiveProxy(receive=request.receive, cached_body=body)
    return body


@dataclasses.dataclass
class ReceiveProxy:
    """Proxy to starlette.types.Receive.__call__ with caching first receive call."""

    receive: starlette.types.Receive
    cached_body: bytes
    _is_first_call: ClassVar[bool] = True

    async def __call__(self):
        # First call will be for getting request body => returns cached result
        if self._is_first_call:
            self._is_first_call = False
            return {"type": "http.request", "body": self.cached_body, "more_body": False}

        return await self.receive()
```

title: Routed paths must always start with /
body:
Hi

Let's say I have the following API:

```
GET /accounts  - list of accounts
POST /accounts  - create account
GET /accounts/{id} - get account by id
DELETE  /accounts/{id} - delete account
```

then I would make a module accounts with APIRouter

in main.py


`app.include_router(accounts.router, prefix="/accounts")`

and in accounts.py

router = APIRouter()


```
@router.get("")
def list_accounts():
    ...

@router.post("")
def list_accounts():
    ...

@router.get("/{id}")
def get_account(id:str):

but I'm getting an error "Routed paths must always start with /"

if I add to router "/" - then my resources will look like  `GET /accounts/` ,  `POST /accounts/`  (but I do not want slash at the end...)


Basically the idea is to put 5-10 methods inside module and all should start with /accounts

title: Header duplicate on subdependency
body:
**Describe the bug**
Getting duplicate headers on API methods with subdependencies requiring the same dependency with a Header argument.

**To Reproduce**
I've created an example repo to reproduce https://github.com/henriklindgren/fastapi-header-bug
Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. Create an API-method which uses the same dependency, with a Header argument, in each of 2 paths of the dependency tree. Like this https://github.com/henriklindgren/fastapi-header-bug/blob/master/app/main.py
2. Visit generated OpenApi json and see duplicate header requirements for the method. 
```json
"parameters": [
  {
    "required": true,
    "schema": {
      "title": "Someheader",
      "type": "string"
    },
    "name": "someheader",
    "in": "header"
  },
  {
    "required": true,
    "schema": {
      "title": "Someheader",
      "type": "string"
    },
    "name": "someheader",
    "in": "header"
  }
]
```

**Expected behavior**
A single header requirement shown in the OpenApi definition.

**Environment:**
 - OS: Linux
 - FastAPI Version: 0.33.0
 - Python Version: 3.7.3
author: henriklindgren

comments.author: dmontagu
dmontagu
comments.body:
I figured out the source of the issue -- it's because during openapi spec generation, the raw flat_dependant is used, which includes duplicate dependencies (which may be cached or not at runtime). However, I can't imagine a scenario where this distinction is relevant externally -- whether the dependency is cached or not, you can't provide distinct inputs for each repeated (uncached) call.

I created a PR (#417) that, when building the openapi schema, uses the caching logic to build a flat dependant that won't replicate dependencies that would have the same cache key. (It does not affect the generation of the flat dependant as it is used during actual route execution.)

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
@henriklindgren thanks for reporting it!

Great job @dmontagu ! :open_mouth: :clap: :tada: Thanks for debugging and fixing it.

The fix is now available in FastAPI `0.36.0`.


---

I'll close this issue now, but feel free to add more comments or issues.

title: OpenAPI: response keys are limited to be status codes
body:
**Describe the bug**
According to OpenAPI 3 specification, under `responses` dictionary each definition starts with the keys which is either a valid status codes (200, 204, 404, etc.), a [range of codes](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/describing-responses#status-codes) (1XX, 2XX, 3XX, etc.) or the word ["default"](https://swagger.io/docs/specification/describing-responses#default).

If a path is defined with such response, the server crashes when opening the doc, redoc or openapi.json URLs.

**To Reproduce**
```python
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from starlette import status
from . import schemas

app = FastAPI()

@app.get('/customer/{id}',
            status_code=status.HTTP_200_OK,
            response_model=schemas.CustomerDetailsResponse,
            responses={
                '4xx': {'model': schemas.ErrorResponse},
                '5xx': {'model': schemas.ErrorResponse},
            },
            )
def customer_details(id: str):
    raise HTTPException(status_code=status.HTTP_501_NOT_IMPLEMENTED)
```

**Expected behavior**
The openapi.json is created, documentation opens, and the responses show the range / default 
definitions.

```yaml
# ...
"/api/customer/{id}":
  get:
    responses:
      '200':
        description: Successful Response
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              "$ref": "#/components/schemas/CustomerDetailsResponse"
      4xx:
        description: Additional Response
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              "$ref": "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"
      5xx:
        description: Additional Response
        content:
          application/json:
            schema:
              "$ref": "#/components/schemas/ErrorResponse"
    summary: Customer Details
    operationId: customer_details_api_customer__id__get
    parameters:
    - required: true
      schema:
        title: Id
        type: string
      name: id
      in: path
```

**Environment:**
 - OS: masOS 10.14.6
 - Python: 3.6.5
 - FastAPI: 0.35.0
author: divums

title: OpenAPI: HTTP_422 response does not use custom media_type
author: divums

title: OpenAPI: media_types of Body parameters and requestBody do not match
body:
**Describe the bug**
The openapi.json for requestBody definitions uses the FastAPI default media_type instead of the defined one in the Body parameter(s).

**To Reproduce**
Create a path with request body that has either a single embedded parameter or multiple ones. List media_type that is different than the default application/json is. 

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette import status
from . import schemas

app = FastAPI()

@app.post('/customers',
         status_code=status.HTTP_201_CREATED,
         )
def customer_create(data: schemas.Customer = Body(..., media_type='application/vnd+json.api', embed=True)):
    created_customer = {**data.dict(), **{'id': '1'}}
    return {'data': created_customer}
```

Then verify your openapi.json.

```yaml
# ...
post:
  responses:
    # ...
  summary: Customer Create
  operationId: customer_create_api_customers_post
  requestBody:
    content:
      application/json: # this should be different
        schema:
          "$ref": "#/components/schemas/Body_customer_create_api_customers_post"
    required: true
```

**Expected behavior**
The media_type of the requestBody of given path matches the specified one:

```yaml
# ...
post:
  responses:
    # ...
  summary: Customer Create
  operationId: customer_create_api_customers_post
  requestBody:
    content:
      application/vnd+json.api:
        schema:
          "$ref": "#/components/schemas/Body_customer_create_api_customers_post"
    required: true
```

**Environment:**
 - OS: masOS 10.14.6
 - Python: 3.6.5
 - FastAPI: 0.35.0
author: divums

title: Using openapi-generator to generate clients results in redundant and repetitive function call names
body:
**Describe the bug**
Using openapi-generator to generate clients from FastAPI projects results in redundant and repetitive function call names

**To Reproduce**
Steps to reproduce the behaviour:
1. Create a REST API with FastAPI
2. Install openapi-generator (https://github.com/OpenAPITools/openapi-generator)
3. Generate a client (I generated a javascript client, for example)
4. Open the documentation and look at the bizarre function calls like: "deleteClaimClaimsClaimIdDelete(claimId)" for a DELETE method against /Claims/{claim_id}

**Expected behaviour**
The function names should not be repetitive and redundant, and should only be based on the action. For example, the DELETE method against /Claims/{claim_id} should follow the Python function name "delete_claim", and therefore show up as "deleteClaim" when converted to camelCase by openapi-generator.

**Environment:**
 - FastAPI Version "==0.33.0"
- Python version "3.7"

**Recommended Fix**
Generation of the "operationId" when creating the "openapi.json" file should be strictly the function name.
author: christopherthompson81

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@christopherthompson81 
I think having this as the default behavior is reasonable because it ensures that there are *never* conflicts in the names that don't stem from conflicts in routes, and that the defaults still include the function name. (I believe the default `operation_id` includes the full route path, the method, and the function name, which is why it looks so redundant.)

However, this is very easy to override manually. I use the following function to clean up the function names in my generated clients (and just take the responsibility on myself to avoid naming conflicts):
```python
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, FastAPI

def setup_openapi(app: FastAPI) -> None:
    """
    Simplify operation IDs so that generated clients have simpler api function names.

    Should be called on a FastAPI instance only after all routes have been added.
    """
    for route in app.routes:
        if isinstance(route, APIRoute):
            route.operation_id = route.name
```

comments.author: christopherthompson81
comments.body:
That does work. Thank-you! I will note that the necessary import is:

```python
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
```

and that the function to redefine the operation_id should be called after setting up the routes.

I also think this might be important information to include in the tutorial related to extending OpenAPI.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @christopherthompson81 updated in the example above. I think a PR adding a note about this to the docs would be well-received.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @dmontagu for the help!

@SKalt added it to the docs in #642 

So I think this is solved, right?

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Also, for what it's worth, I've added this to [fastapi-utils](https://fastapi-utils.davidmontague.xyz/user-guide/openapi/) in case you'd rather rely on someone else to maintain its implementation and not copy/paste it into each project.

title: FastAPI exceptions module mistakenly references the 'requests' package
body:
**Describe the bug**
Starting up a FastAPI 0.38.0 app displays the following error:

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/__init__.py", line 7, in <module>
    from .applications import FastAPI
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/applications.py", line 3, in <module>
    from fastapi import routing
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/routing.py", line 7, in <module>
    from fastapi.dependencies.models import Dependant
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/dependencies/models.py", line 3, in <module>
    from fastapi.security.base import SecurityBase
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/security/__init__.py", line 2, in <module>
    from .http import (
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/security/http.py", line 5, in <module>
    from fastapi.exceptions import HTTPException
  File ".../lib/site-packages/fastapi/exceptions.py", line 5, in <module>
    from requests import Request
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'
```

**Expected behavior**
The app should start without import errors.

**Environment:**
 - OS: Linux, Windows, and macOS
 - FastAPI Version: 0.38.0

**Additional context**
It's likely the `from requests import Request` should be replaced with `from starlette.requests import Request` in line 5 of `fastapi/exceptions.py`

title: msgpack response if client asks for application/x-msgpack
body:
hi,
we are in the process of evaling fastapi for a large app migration from flask. Quick question on a very interesting and useful piece of functionality. 
This builds upon the validations you have created for encoding json data and will be very useful for us out-of-the-box.

https://msgpack.org/index.html is an enhancement to json which is highly compressed. There are some benchmarks that show it is as fast as protocol buffers...while making it a drop-in alternative to json. 

msgpack is very important for Asia which are highly internet bandwidth constrained. We are prepared to tradeoff speed in exchange for lesser bandwidth (e.g. http://indiegamr.com/cut-your-data-exchange-traffic-by-up-to-50-with-one-line-of-code-msgpack-vs-json/ )

To be fair, it may be same size as gzipped json responses...but after having done android app deployments over a million devices in the field in asia, we realized that one has to write custom compression/decompression for gzipped json response handling. However msgpack has fairly well supported libraries. 

msgpack is fairly production ready. Lots of downstream python libraries use it in production - https://github.com/0rpc/zerorpc-python

This enhancement request is for fastapi to respond in msgpack if the requesting client ask for the mimetype `application/x-msgpack`. The default behavior continues to be the same as currently.

There is no impact to any client of a fastapi deployment unless they specifically ask for msgpack (in which case, they will get better performance).

I'm also pasting some criticisms of msgpack and comparisons with competing/derived json formats like CBOR

- https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=14067747
- http://zderadicka.eu/comparison-of-json-like-serializations-json-vs-ubjson-vs-messagepack-vs-cbor/
- https://techtutorialsx.com/2019/06/20/esp32-arduinojson-messagepack-serialization/
- https://gist.github.com/frsyuki/2908191
- https://prataprc.github.io/msgpack-vs-cbor.html
author: sandys

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys 
I think fastapi may actually be relatively well suited to handle this now without modifications.

If your top priority is just reducing bandwidth usage, I think it should be straightforward to implement converting the json responses to msgpack (in the presence of the appropriate mimetype request) as an ASGI middleware. I imagine that would involve parsing the json and dumping it to msgpack format, so would come with some processing overhead. But that overhead would probably be negligible compared to network, especially if bandwidth is the source of the concern.

If you really want to avoid the reparsing, I think this would also be possible by creating a custom starlette response that holds the python dict you want to serialize (rather than encoded json), and adding a middleware that converts responses of that type to a new starlette.Response with msgpack-encoded contents.

If you are serious about this and want/need a little more guidance than provided above, I can put together more of a skeleton implementation.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
This might be even easier than I thought.

I'd suggest taking a look at `starlette.responses.JSONResponse` -- it looks like you could pretty easily create a `MsgpackResponse` by defining an appropriate `render` function (e.g., using `msgpack-python`). Then, you could have your endpoints return `msgpack`-encoded data by setting `response_class=MsgpackResponse`. That wouldn't allow for dynamic determination of the response type based on the request, but maybe there is still a way to achieve that...

(Certainly, in the worst case, you could just perform a reparsing of the `JSONResponse` in a middleware.)

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys
Actually, I got interested in this and was able to throw together a working example -- not quite how I expected it would need to go, and it has some hacks that could probably be removed (maybe would require a feature request or two), but it works:

```python
from typing import Any, Dict, List, Type

import msgpack
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_app
from starlette.background import BackgroundTask
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse, Response
from starlette.routing import request_response
from starlette.testclient import TestClient


class MsgpackResponse(Response):
    media_type = "application/msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MultiResponse(JSONResponse):
    serialization_header = "Accept"
    default_response_class = JSONResponse
    response_class_map: Dict[str, Type[Response]] = {"application/x-msgpack": MsgpackResponse}

    def __init__(
        self,
        content: Any = None,
        status_code: int = 200,
        headers: dict = None,
        media_type: str = None,
        background: BackgroundTask = None,
    ) -> None:
        self.content = content
        self._status_code = status_code
        self._media_type = media_type
        self._background = background
        self.init_headers(headers)

    def for_request(self, request: Request) -> Response:
        serialization_method = request.headers.get(self.serialization_header)
        response_class = self.response_class_map.get(
            serialization_method, self.default_response_class
        )
        return response_class(
            content=self.content,
            status_code=self._status_code,
            headers=self.headers,
            media_type=self._media_type,
            background=self._background,
        )


class MultiAPIRoute(APIRoute):
    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        if kwargs["response_class"] is JSONResponse:
            kwargs["response_class"] = MultiResponse
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        # Copied below from the end of the APIRoute init; overwriting the self.app with a modified version
        base_app_func = get_app(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            response_class=self.response_class,
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_skip_defaults=self.response_model_skip_defaults,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

        async def app_func(request: Request) -> Response:
            response = await base_app_func(request)
            if isinstance(response, MultiResponse):
                response = response.for_request(request)
            return response

        self.app = request_response(app_func)


app = FastAPI()
app.router.route_class = MultiAPIRoute


@app.get("/", response_model=List[int])
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]


client = TestClient(app)
print(client.get("/").content)
# b'[1,2,3]'

print(client.get("/", headers={"X-Serialization": "msgpack"}).content)
# b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
```

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys 

I've reworked it to be substantially simpler, and also I've put some thought into what first-class support could/should look like in FastAPI.

-----

Here's a msgpack-specific simplified implementation (with minimal docs) that runs as is to show how simple it is:
(note `include_both_serialization_routes` could be removed with a relatively minor feature request, described below).
<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
    from typing import Any, List, Tuple

    import msgpack
    from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
    from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
    from starlette.requests import Request
    from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
    from starlette.routing import Match
    from starlette.testclient import TestClient
    from starlette.types import Scope

    class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
        media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

        def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
            return msgpack.packb(content)

    class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
        detection_header = "Accept"
        detection_header_match = "appliaction/x-msgpack"
        response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
        include_in_schema_override = False

        def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
            include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
            response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])
            kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
            kwargs["response_class"] = response_class
            super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
            if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
                return Match.NONE, {}
            return super().matches(scope)

    def include_both_serialization_routes(
        parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter
    ) -> None:
        original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
        parent_router.route_class = MsgpackRoute
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)

    router = APIRouter()
    @router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
    async def root():
        return [1, 2, 3]

    app = FastAPI()
    include_both_serialization_routes(app.router, router)
    client = TestClient(app)

    print(client.get("/", headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'

    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'
```
</details>

-----

Next, here's a more general implementation that might be worthy of first-class support (**note:** it should run as is if you copy/paste):
<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
    from typing import Any, List, Optional, ClassVar, get_type_hints, Tuple, Type
    
    import msgpack
    from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
    from fastapi.routing import APIRoute
    from starlette.requests import Request
    from starlette.responses import JSONResponse
    from starlette.routing import Match
    from starlette.testclient import TestClient
    from starlette.types import Scope
    
    class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
        media_type = "application/x-msgpack"
    
        def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
            return msgpack.packb(content)
    
    class OverrideResponseClassRoute(APIRoute):
        detection_header: ClassVar[str]
        detection_header_match: ClassVar[str]
        response_class_mapping: ClassVar[JSONResponse]
        include_in_schema_override: ClassVar[Optional[bool]] = None
    
        def __init_subclass__(cls) -> None:
            missing_attributes: List[str] = []
            for attribute in get_type_hints(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
                if not hasattr(cls, attribute):
                    missing_attributes.append(attribute)
            if missing_attributes:
                raise TypeError(f"{cls.__name__} did not define the following attributes: {missing_attributes}")
    
        def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
            include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
            response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])
    
            kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
            kwargs["response_class"] = response_class
    
            super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
    
        def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
            if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
                return Match.NONE, {}
            return super().matches(scope)
    
    class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
        detection_header = "Accept"
        detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
        response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
        include_in_schema_override = False
    
    def include_typed_router(
        parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
    ) -> None:
        original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
        parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
        try:
            parent_router.include_router(include_router)
        finally:
            parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
    
    router = APIRouter()
    @router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
    async def root():
        return [1, 2, 3]

    app = FastAPI()
    include_typed_router(app.router, router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
    include_typed_router(app.router, router)
    client = TestClient(app)
    
    print(client.get("/", headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
    
    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'
```
</details>

-----

Discussion of appropriate feature requests
-----

```python
class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)
```
Getting this "built in" would probably require the following two feature requests:
* starlette: Add this class as a Response (NOT as a subclass of JSONResponse)
* fastapi: Allow non-None response_model if using a non-subclass of JSONResponse

(I think it is unlikely that fastapi would add this directly, but I suppose it's possible.)

Given how short it is, I expect it may be better not to upstream it, and have to deal with the additional testing burden, etc.

-----

```python
class OverrideResponseClassRoute(APIRoute):
    detection_header: ClassVar[str]
    detection_header_match: ClassVar[str]
    response_class_mapping: ClassVar[JSONResponse]
    include_in_schema_override: ClassVar[Optional[bool]] = None

    def __init_subclass__(cls) -> None:
        missing_attributes: List[str] = []
        for attribute in get_type_hints(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
            if not hasattr(cls, attribute):
                missing_attributes.append(attribute)
        if missing_attributes:
            raise TypeError(f"{cls.__name__} did not define the following attributes: {missing_attributes}")

    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        """
        This could be made more typesafe by repeating all the args/kwargs explicitly.
        """
        include_in_schema = kwargs["include_in_schema"] if self.include_in_schema_override is None else None
        response_class = self.response_class_mapping.get(kwargs["response_class"], kwargs["response_class"])

        kwargs["include_in_schema"] = include_in_schema
        kwargs["response_class"] = response_class

        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def matches(self, scope: Scope) -> Tuple[Match, Scope]:
        if not Request(scope).headers.get(self.detection_header) == self.detection_header_match:
            return Match.NONE, {}
        return super().matches(scope)

class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
    detection_header = "Accept"
    detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
    response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
    include_in_schema_override = False
```
@sandys is this what you had in mind for having the client specify the mimetype? One might want to parse the accept header into a list of strings, but this works for now.

-----

```python
def include_typed_router(
    parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
) -> None:
    original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
    parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
    try:
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
    finally:
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class
```
This function could be removed if fastapi supported `route_class` parameter in APIRouter.include_router. I personally think this is a reasonable feature request that should be added.

-----

With the above feature requests, even without built-in support for msgpack, the ultimate usage would be as simple as:
```python
# ###################
# ##### msgpack #####
# ###################
class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/msgpack"
    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)

class MsgpackRoute(OverrideResponseClassRoute):
    detection_header = "Accept"
    detection_header_match = "application/x-msgpack"
    response_class_mapping = {JSONResponse: MsgpackResponse}
    include_in_schema_override = False
# ###################

router = APIRouter()
@router.get("/", response_model=List[int])
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]

app = FastAPI()
app.include_router(router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
app.include_router(router)
```
@sandys Would this be good enough for you to consider it as being supported in a "first class" way? If not, is there anything specific you don't like, or think is missing?

Edit: I suppose it would be good to add support for receiving msgpack payloads as well; this would probably need to be done in a middleware.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Rereading your original comment, I think I see more explicitly what you are looking for:

> This enhancement request is for fastapi to respond in msgpack if the requesting client ask for the mimetype application/x-msgpack.

I didn't think about the idea that this would just be handled by default for all apps. I think this could be done inside FastAPI using an approach similar to that proposed above, and could be an option to enable if msgpack is present. But I don't know if it's worth the additional maintenance burden on fastapi; I don't know how commonly used this serialization format is (especially compared to JSON).

@tiangolo If I had to guess, I would assume that this feature request is outside the scope of what you'd want to support out of the box. But if that's wrong, let me know and I can take a stab at a PR incorporating this directly into FastAPI.

comments.body:
@dmontagu i would like to again add a million thanks to the work you have done above. I do not think i would have been able to figure this stuff out without putting weeks into it...and maybe still not (or would have given up).

And that is a good context to why this is important. I come from the Rails world and I frequently read about how big sites use Rails (e.g. [here](https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=20920555)) . The key to observe here is "batteries included" but not linked to the batteries.

For example in Rails you can skip all the "batteries included" functionality by opt-out. Rails is opt-in by default ...and that is what im asking Fastapi devs to consider. 

`rails new app_name --skip-action-cable --skip-action-text `

Fastapi is quickly becoming more than just a wrapper on top of starlette. And that is a tradeoff between functionality versus simplicity (and even performance). For example Starlette still beats Fastapi on performance, but for a developer, there is no question which he will choose. It may come as no surprise that most people start with https://github.com/tiangolo/full-stack-fastapi-postgresql . I personally would not have been able to begin with Fastapi if i didnt have that starter readymade. 

 I think (and I really hope) that Fastapi keeps adding batteries , which are skippable. Because i know for sure - that without you writing the code above, there was an almost zero chance that I would have achieved it myself in time.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys I ended up having to go back to the older implementation to get it to work. I did have to make use of a private API on the request to replace its body (to prepare it to be consumed by pydantic). This could be handled without the override of the body, but it would involve a small change to a very long method in fastapi, somewhere near this line:
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/580cf8f4e2aac3d4f298fbb3ca1426f9ea6265de/fastapi/routing.py#L93

I opted for the small change to a short(er) method instead.

```python
import json
from typing import Any, Optional, Type, Dict

import msgpack
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse, Response
from starlette.routing import request_response
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

from fastapi import FastAPI, APIRouter
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_app


class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    def __init__(self, *args: Any, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        original_app = get_app(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            response_class=self.response_class,
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_skip_defaults=self.response_model_skip_defaults,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

        async def app(request: Request) -> Response:
            if request.headers.get("Content-Type") == "application/x-msgpack":
                body = await request.body()
                request._body = json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(body))).encode()
            response = await original_app(request)
            if request.headers.get("Accept") == "application/x-msgpack":
                return MsgpackResponse(content=json.loads(response.body))
            return response

        self.app = request_response(app)


def include_typed_router(
    parent_router: APIRouter, include_router: APIRouter, *, route_class: Optional[Type[APIRoute]] = None
) -> None:
    original_route_class = parent_router.route_class
    parent_router.route_class = route_class or original_route_class
    try:
        parent_router.include_router(include_router)
    finally:
        parent_router.route_class = original_route_class


router = APIRouter()


@router.post("/")
async def root(body: Dict[str, Any]):
    return body


app = FastAPI()
include_typed_router(app.router, router, route_class=MsgpackRoute)
client = TestClient(app)

input = {"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": "c"}
msgpack_out_response = client.post("/", json=input, headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content
print(msgpack_out_response)
# b'\x82\xa1a\x93\x01\x02\x03\xa1b\xa1c'
assert jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(msgpack_out_response)) == input

msgpack_in_response = client.post(
    "/", data=msgpack_out_response, headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-msgpack"}
).content
print(msgpack_in_response)
# b'{"a":[1,2,3],"b":"c"}'
assert json.loads(msgpack_in_response) == input
```

This approach has the benefit of not adding extra routes to be matched.

Let me know if this implementation works for you.

-----

Going through this actually also revealed to me another good feature request for FastAPI: the ability to easily override the way the `self.app` is generated in the route class. This would make it easier to add header-specific route behavior by using a subclass of APIRoute. I'll throw together some PRs that would reduce the amount of custom code involved, but even if nothing is accepted the above example should still work for you.

I’m not sure if this covers the problem of conditionally encoding the *response* in a middleware-like wrapper yet, but it will handle the case where the *request* contains msgpack. comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys Yes, that is what I meant. The docs won't always show everything that is possible, but you shouldn't let that discourage you from trying to adapt the examples to more complex scenarios.

<details>
    <summary>Click to expand</summary>

```python
import json
from typing import Callable, List, Any, Awaitable

import msgpack
from starlette.testclient import TestClient
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response, JSONResponse

from fastapi import Body, FastAPI
from fastapi.encoders import jsonable_encoder
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute

HandlerType = Callable[[Request], Awaitable[Response]]
MSGPACK_LABEL = "application/x-msgpack"


class MsgpackRequest(Request):
    async def body(self) -> bytes:
        if not hasattr(self, "_body"):
            body = await super().body()
            if MSGPACK_LABEL in self.headers.getlist("Content-Type"):
                body = json.dumps(jsonable_encoder(msgpack.loads(body))).encode()
            self._body = body
        return self._body


class MsgpackResponse(JSONResponse):
    media_type = MSGPACK_LABEL

    def render(self, content: Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)

    @classmethod
    def from_response(cls, response: Response) -> "MsgpackResponse":
        content = json.loads(response.body)
        msgpack_response = MsgpackResponse(content, response.status_code, background=response.background)
        headers = response.headers
        for key, value in msgpack_response.headers.items():  # reset content-type and content-length
            headers[key] = value
        msgpack_response._headers = headers
        return msgpack_response


def msgpack_route_handler(handler: HandlerType) -> HandlerType:
    """
    Implemented as a decorator for easier composition; could be combined with gzip, for example
    """

    async def msgpack_handler(request: Request) -> Response:
        request = MsgpackRequest(request.scope, request.receive)
        response = await handler(request)
        if MSGPACK_LABEL in request.headers.getlist("Accept"):
            return MsgpackResponse.from_response(response)
        return response

    return msgpack_handler


class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    def get_route_handler(self) -> Callable:
        route_handler = super().get_route_handler()
        route_handler = msgpack_route_handler(route_handler)
        return route_handler


app = FastAPI()
app.router.route_class = MsgpackRoute


@app.post("/sum")
async def sum_numbers(numbers: List[int] = Body(...)):
    return {"sum": sum(numbers)}

# Test that it works:

client = TestClient(app)


def msgpack_request(use_msgpack: bool, accept_msgpack: bool):
    n = 5
    headers = {}
    body = [1] * n
    if use_msgpack:
        data = msgpack.packb(body)
        headers["Content-Type"] = MSGPACK_LABEL
    else:
        data = json.dumps(body).encode()
    if accept_msgpack:
        headers["Accept"] = MSGPACK_LABEL
    response = client.post("/sum", data=data, headers=headers)
    if accept_msgpack:
        assert response.headers["Content-Type"] == MSGPACK_LABEL
        response_json = msgpack.loads(response.content, raw=False)
    else:
        response_json = response.json()
    assert response_json == {"sum": n}
    print(f"{data} -> {response.content}")


def confirm_it_works():
    for use_msgpack in [True, False]:
        for accept_msgpack in [True, False]:
            msgpack_request(use_msgpack=use_msgpack, accept_msgpack=accept_msgpack)
    print("Success")


confirm_it_works()

"""
b'\x95\x01\x01\x01\x01\x01' -> b'\x81\xa3sum\x05'
b'\x95\x01\x01\x01\x01\x01' -> b'{"sum":5}'
b'[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]' -> b'\x81\xa3sum\x05'
b'[1, 1, 1, 1, 1]' -> b'{"sum":5}'
Success
"""
```
</details>

This change basically allows you to use a handler-specific "middleware" that interacts with the request and response. (Note that the above approach can have issues with composing the requests, since only the `body()` logic of the last request is maintained.)
