comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
Not sure about the current state about a potential `MsgPackResponse` class here, but my thinking goes that adding it to Starlette rather than FastAPI would make it more widely available, since many applications and frameworks (FastAPI) depend on Starlette.

In fact, looking at the app-wrapping logic in some of the snippets here, an ASGI `MsgPackMiddleware` shipped with Starlette (similar to [`GZipMiddleware`](https://www.starlette.io/middleware/#gzipmiddleware)) would definitely be even more appropriate and benefit the wider community even more, as it would be usable with any ASGI application.

(In general, I think any solution that embraces ASGI as the common compatibility interface should be considered first, rather than framework-specific solutions.)

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body: Reading more about this issue and others, it seems there has been a variety of discussions around parsing the request body in various formats (gzip, msgpack, others), which Starlette currently does seem to support, and which isn't an ASGI-level piece of functionality. So my previous comment about an ASGI middleware *might* be misguided.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
> So my previous comment about an ASGI middleware *might* be misguided.

Actually, it was not. ;-)

I pushed [msgpack-asgi](https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi), an ASGI middleware that provides drop-in MessagePack support to/from JSON.

FastAPI example based on the one in the issue description (using HTTPX is not mandatory, I just happen to have it installed locally instead of Requests):

```python
import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI
from msgpack_asgi.middleware import MessagePackMiddleware

app = FastAPI()
app.add_middleware(MessagePackMiddleware)


@app.get("/")
async def root():
    return [1, 2, 3]


with httpx.Client(app=app, base_url="http://testserver") as client:
    print(client.get("/").content)
    # b'[1,2,3]'

    print(client.get("/", headers={"accept": "application/x-msgpack"}).content)
    # b'\x93\x01\x02\x03'
```

Should also work with Starlette and any other ASGI apps!

It also has an undocumented `MessagePackResponse` response class for cases when we'd want to explicitly send MessagePack data, though I'm not sure whether it is actually useful compared to the middleware.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
This is awesome!

It looks like you've handled most of the typical concerns with having middleware access the request body (e.g., not encoding a streaming response).

I'm wondering if maybe there is a way to encapsulate most/all of those checks into some kind of `BodyMiddleware` base class that has hooks for reading and/or updating the request and  response bodies. That might reduce the need for the `get_route_handler` method on `APIRoute`, as the main reason for its introduction was to fill the gap in access to the request/response bodies in traditional middleware.

Adding the `get_route_handler` was my own suggestion/contribution (with lots of input/help from @tiangolo of course), but it does feel to me like more of a workaround than a first class solution. If your approach to handling the bodies in middleware is more consistent with the rest of the ASGI ecosystem, it might make sense to stop recommending the use of `get_route_handler`.

------

Also related, I'm wondering if it could make sense to make it easier to add middlewares to specific routes and/or routers.

If the `get_route_handler` was replaced or augmented with a `get_app` function that applies the `request_response` to the result of `get_route_handler` *along* with any desired middleware, that could serve as the foundation for the ability to apply specific middlewares only to specific routes or routers, rather than the whole application, which seems like a relatively common request.

Alternatively, if it were easier to merge the openapi schemas of mounted subapps into the parent app, then I think selective middleware could be managed just through the use of different `FastAPI` instances when you want different middlewares.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
> This is awesome!

Cheers!

> It looks like you've handled most of the typical concerns with having middleware access the request body (e.g., not encoding a streaming response).

Well, not really, since currently `msgpack-asgi` does not support streaming responses (it raises a `NotImplementedError`). The main reason is that I'm not too sure what the semantics of a "streaming MessagePack response" would be, honestly. (It might make sense to stream individual MessagePack objects, in which case [Unpacker](https://github.com/msgpack/msgpack-python#streaming-unpacking) could be useful, but I don't even think that's a common pattern.)

(This makes me realize that `MessagePackMiddleware` currently consumes the full request body in order to replace it with the JSON-re-encoded version. I should definitely impose the same restriction than on responses, because malicious clients could keep sending more data and fill the RAM very easily. Edit: fixed in https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi/commit/b1fe8fbcba14d973cfc4f337251b942660ca8639)

So yeah, there are still some rough edges around streaming. :-) (and these might be unavoidable on the request side, as @tomchristie suggested in https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495#issuecomment-494008175).

> I'm wondering if maybe there is a way to encapsulate most/all of those checks into some kind of `BodyMiddleware` base class

I think a simple form of this that consumes the request body and applies a transform function on it before passing it on is probably possible, yes, though it's probably not going to be optimal in most cases.

Here's a working implementation, though:

```python
import functools
import typing

from starlette.datastructures import Headers
from starlette.types import ASGIApp, Receive, Send, Scope, Message

BodyTransformer = typing.Callable[[bytes, Headers], bytes]


class RequestBodyMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, app: ASGIApp, transform: BodyTransformer):
        self.app = app
        self.transform = transform

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        if scope["type"] != "http":
            await self.app(scope, receive, send)
            return
        app = RequestBodyResponder(self.app, transform=self.transform)
        await app(scope, receive, send)


class RequestBodyResponder:
    def __init__(
        self, app: ASGIApp, transform: BodyTransformer = lambda body, _: body,
    ):
        self.app = app
        self.transform = transform
        self.initial_message: Message = {}
        self.headers = Headers()

    async def __call__(self, scope: Scope, receive: Receive, send: Send) -> None:
        assert scope["type"] == "http"
        self.headers = Headers(scope=scope)
        receive = functools.partial(self._receive, receive)
        await self.app(scope, receive, send)

    async def _receive(self, receive: Receive) -> Message:
        body = b""
        more_body = True
        while more_body:
            message = await receive()
            assert message["type"] == "http.request"
            body += message["body"]
            more_body = message.get("more_body", False)

        message["body"] = self.transform(body, self.headers)

        return message
```

Example usage for the GZip request body use case documented [here](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/custom-request-and-route/#handling-custom-request-body-encodings):

```python
import gzip

from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.datastructures import Headers
from pydantic import BaseModel

from .request_body_middleware import RequestBodyMiddleware

app = FastAPI()


def gzip_decompress(body: bytes, headers: Headers) -> bytes:
    if "gzip" in headers.getlist("Content-Encoding"):
        return gzip.decompress(body)
    return body


app.add_middleware(RequestBodyMiddleware, transform=gzip_decompress)


class Body(BaseModel):
    message: str


@app.post("/")
async def root(body: Body):
    return {"body": body}
```

Test with:

```bash
echo '{"message": "test"}' | gzip | curl -i http://localhost:8000 -H 'Content-Encoding: gzip' --data-binary @-
```

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
date: Fri, 01 Nov 2019 20:13:18 GMT
server: uvicorn
content-length: 27
content-type: application/json

{"body":{"message":"test"}}
```

I wrote earlier "the implementation is not going to be optimal in most cases". In the GZip case, it is not optimal because the middleware is going to load the whole request body into memory before passing it to the application — regardless of whether the application uses `await request.body()` or `await request.stream()`. We could implement a more subtle solution that maintains a `BytesIO` buffer and a `GZipFile` (much like what `GZipMiddleware` does, but in reverse), but that would probably mean adding extra hooks and APIs that more simple use cases won't need.

In the end, building a performant specialized middleware is probably better than having a potentially sloppy generic middleware. :-)

(Also, I only mentioned processing requests here because building a generic middleware that *also* supports transforming the response is even more subtle. There are a few corner cases that need to be handled e.g. updating the `Content-Length`, having support for changing the `Content-Type`, and then the same kind of issues with handling a streaming body.)

---

@dmontagu If the APIs added to FastAPI to support modifying the request body mainly aimed at adding support for GZip request bodies (an issue also mentioned in https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/644), an alternative worth considering is working on extending the `GZipMiddleware` shipped with Starlette to also support decoding gzipped requests (with support for streaming etc. as hinted above).

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@florimondmanca 

> Well, not really, since currently msgpack-asgi does not support streaming responses (it raises a NotImplementedError).

That's actually what I meant -- better to raise an error than try to handle it incorrectly. (This is a good argument for more granular applications of middleware though!)

> malicious clients could keep sending more data and fill the RAM very easily

Is this not the case without this middleware? By that I mean., if you were just receiving regular json requests, wouldn't this still be a problem? I think it probably just makes sense to handle this somewhere else (e.g., using a max-request-size limit in your reverse proxy). Unless there's something particularly bad about msgpack's memory usage.

-----

The FastAPI APIs I was talking about were added to fill a hole in functionality related to reading or modifying request/response bodies in middleware. There were actually a number of distinct issues this addressed, including custom encoding/decoding (like gzip and msgpack), but also related to broad-spectrum logging of request bodies, which had been requested on multiple occasions.

> In the GZip case, it is not optimal because the middleware is going to load the whole request body into memory before passing it to the application

Yeah, this is problematic if streaming is integral to your application, but it seems to me that that represents a very small fraction of fastapi usage compared to just wanting to handle individual http requests.

I actually think your generic middleware here would address a lot of otherwise painful issues, though it would help if it were easier to apply only to specific routes (e.g., on a router-by-router basis).

As long as routes/routers implement ASGI, I can see no theoretical issue with applying middleware to specific routes only. :-) Not sure what the FastAPI way of doing it would be, though.

I’m not too keen on the idea, sorry.

Generally, mostly for ideological reasons around the agnosticism of ASGI, but also to keep the scope and maintainability cost low, I’m not very keen on making specific frameworks come through too much (if at all) in these kinds of projects.

FWIW there’s already a note there that “usage should be adapted to the user’s specific ASGI framework”.

My rationale here really is: if we add an example for FastAPI then out of fairness we should at least add one for Starlette, which now has a different middleware API entirely. And that brings us down the “coupling with downstream frameworks/contents” path... whereas I believe it should be the other way around.

comments.author: jcsaaddupuy
comments.body:
Hi,

I know this issue have been closed but I came accross the need to have custom requets / responses parsing, specifically in msgpack.
This could be extended to any content types in the requests headers accept / content-type.

Based on all the comments here (specifically this [one](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/521#issuecomment-532043464) , thanks @dmontagu) + some other insights ( https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1450) I found a solution that works, without any underlying json usage.

The idea behind using `msgpack` is because it's _way faster_ than `json`.

Implementing `msgpack` support without eliminating underlying json dumps / load in the code by using a middleware  make the use of msgpack _useless_ from a performance point of view.

In this implementation, the `MsgpackRequest` override the `json()` method to play nicely with the rest of the request processing, but without any conversion to `json`.

```python
import time
import typing as t

import msgpack
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_request_handler
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class MsgpackRequest(Request):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    async def json(self) -> bytes:
        if not hasattr(self, "_json"):
            body = await super().body()
            self._json = msgpack.unpackb(body)
        return self._json


class MsgpackResponse(Response):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: t.Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)


class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    # keep track of content-type -> request classes
    REQUESTS_CLASSES = {MsgpackRequest.media_type: MsgpackRequest}
    # keep track of content-type -> response classes
    ROUTES_HANDLERS_CLASSES = {MsgpackResponse.media_type: MsgpackResponse}

    def _get_media_type_route_handler(self, media_type):
        return get_request_handler(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            # use custom response class or fallback on default self.response_class
            response_class=self.ROUTES_HANDLERS_CLASSES.get(
                media_type, self.response_class
            ),
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_exclude_unset=self.response_model_exclude_unset,
            response_model_exclude_defaults=self.response_model_exclude_defaults,
            response_model_exclude_none=self.response_model_exclude_none,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

    def get_route_handler(self) -> t.Callable:
        async def custom_route_handler(request: Request) -> Response:

            content_type = request.headers.get("Content-Type")
            try:
                request_cls = self.REQUESTS_CLASSES[content_type]
                request = request_cls(request.scope, request.receive)
            except KeyError:
                # nothing registered to handle content_type, process given requests as-is
                pass

            accept = request.headers.get("Accept")
            route_handler = self._get_media_type_route_handler(accept)
            return await route_handler(request)

        return custom_route_handler
```

I believe this could be generalized for any combination of content-type / accept.

Having the support for custom requests / responses implemented directly in the framework would then be awesome.


While this solution works on success resposnes, fastpi will still respond with json whenever an error is raised.
Do you have any hint to have any status code response being processed y the `MsgpackResponse` ?
As a client for my API, I would expect it to always respond with the proper format asked with the header "Accept", the response being a success or an error.


Let me know if this raise any interest, I would be glad to propose a pull request for this feature.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
> Implementing msgpack support without eliminating underlying json dumps / load in the code by using a middleware make the use of msgpack useless from a performance point of view.

Yes it doesn’t help with speed and CPU usage, but AFAIU it’s not been the motivation for folks using msgpack (at least as I understood it in this thread - but you seem to disagree and I haven’t run or seen benchmarks so I can’t tell). Some people may be okay with paying that small CPU utilization fee, and I’m happy that msgpack-asgi can help folks address those use cases. We both agree that this approach has a CPU cost, but just though I’d point that out. :)

comments.author: jcsaaddupuy
comments.body:
@sandys this is kind of a hacky integration, but except from the direct mspack packing / unpacking part, one feature I would love to see in fastapi would be the ability to register custom requests / responses objects at the FastAPI object level.

This is kind of implemented in the `MsgpackRoute` who keep track of customs Request/Responses depending on the mimetype.

Could be an interface like 

```python
app = FastAPI()
...
app.register_request(content_type, CustomRequestClass)
app.register_respone(content_type, CustomResponseClass)
```

This would allow one to separate totally the parsing of the request / the serialization of the responses from the logic implemented in the routes.

comments.author: estan
estan
comments.body:
Also, regarding

> @sandys I don’t know, but it wouldn’t surprise me if there was no way to get it to appear in the swagger docs — msgpack isn’t really part of the open api spec. But I think it may be possible to make a valid open api spec that includes the right options for content type.

From https://swagger.io/specification/#format:

> Note: While APIs may be defined by OpenAPI documents in either YAML or JSON format, the API request and response bodies and other content are not required to be JSON or YAML.

So while the OpenAPI documents themselves are JSON/YAML, OpenAPI seems to be agnostic about the serialization of the described APIs, even if JSON is for sure the most common.

comments.author: dgasmith
comments.body:
To give an example of msgpack vs JSON speed: a 1MB array of doubles held in python array buffer or NumPy array can be msgpack encoded/decoded 10,000/1,000x faster than JSON as well as being 3-8x smaller depending on the data type and associated numerical entropy of the data. So the CPU usage cost can be quite restrictive in some use cases and I do think this should be mentioned explicitly on the msgpack middleware repo so that practitioners are not confused.

I did a study of the issue [here](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/1450) (as mentioned) and there are only a couple points in the FastAPI structure which prevent multiple encode/decode types from being usable. We typically like to have JSON available for general use or something better for numerical data (msgpack/CBORS/Parquet) depending on the client. I'm happy to make a PR to fix up these issues, but I do wish for some discussion on if its the best way of tackling the problem.

As @estan pointed out the solution should be general based on media type or we run into this issue with the next serialization format to come along.

comments.author: florimondmanca
comments.body:
Hi all,

I pushed a commit to [`msgpack-asgi`](https://github.com/florimondmanca/msgpack-asgi) to clarify the caveats wrt CPU usage:

> This gives you the bandwitdth usage reduction benefits of MessagePack without having to change existing code.
>
> **Note**: this comes at a CPU usage cost, since `MessagePackMiddleware` will perform MsgPack decoding while your application continues to decode and encode JSON data (see also [How it works](#how-it-works)). If your use case is CPU-sensitive, rather than strictly focused on reducing network bandwidth, this package may not be for you.

What is the current best practices for adding msgpack functionality to fastapi (while bypassing serialization to JSON)?

I see from this thread that there are some ways of doing it, but also several accepted pull requests that might render them superfluous workarounds. What is the current idiomatic way to add msgpack?

comments.author: dgasmith
comments.body: We ended up monkey patching FastAPI to allow for custom accept/response headers (as opposed to assuming everything is JSON). I don't believe there is currently an idiomatic way to support this feature.

comments.author: swamidass
comments.body: Thanks @dgasmith. Would you mind please linking to the specific trick that worked for you (which I am assuming is in this thread somewhere?).

comments.author: dgasmith
comments.body:
Two places:
- [Content-type](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L204)
- [Accept-Type](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/fastapi/routing.py#L264)

We also skip `serialize_response` and let the serializers handle cases such as NumPy arrays for speed. This solution can hurt you unless you fully understand the interaction of the field validation and ensure your encoders can handle all edge cases without pydantic helper functions. The results can be extremely fast for complex objects in the pydata ecosystem which benefit from msgpack/feather/parquet however.

comments.author: swamidass
comments.body:
Okay, for anyone who follows, this is the code I was able to get working on fastapi 0.88.0, following @jcsaaddupuy. Note the custom request was not included, because @jcsaaddupuy does not seem to work any more. For some reason, a msgpack request confuses fastapi's typing machinery and it throws an error.

Also, @dgasmith , this approach did not require any monkey patching. Though I suspect it may be brittle for relying on the internals of fastapi...


```

import typing as t
import json
import msgpack
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.routing import APIRoute, get_request_handler
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import Response

from starlette.routing import request_response
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

class MsgpackResponse(Response):
    media_type = "application/x-msgpack"

    def render(self, content: t.Any) -> bytes:
        return msgpack.packb(content)

class MsgpackRoute(APIRoute):
    ROUTES_HANDLERS_CLASSES = {MsgpackResponse.media_type: MsgpackResponse}

    def _get_media_type_route_handler(self, media_type):
        return get_request_handler(
            dependant=self.dependant,
            body_field=self.body_field,
            status_code=self.status_code,
            # use custom response class or fallback on default self.response_class
            response_class=self.ROUTES_HANDLERS_CLASSES.get(
                media_type, self.response_class
            ),
            response_field=self.secure_cloned_response_field,
            response_model_include=self.response_model_include,
            response_model_exclude=self.response_model_exclude,
            response_model_by_alias=self.response_model_by_alias,
            response_model_exclude_unset=self.response_model_exclude_unset,
            response_model_exclude_defaults=self.response_model_exclude_defaults,
            response_model_exclude_none=self.response_model_exclude_none,
            dependency_overrides_provider=self.dependency_overrides_provider,
        )

    def get_route_handler(self) -> t.Callable:
        async def custom_route_handler(request: Request) -> Response:
            accept = request.headers.get("Accept")
            route_handler = self._get_media_type_route_handler(accept)
            return await route_handler(request)

        return custom_route_handler


app = FastAPI()
app.router.route_class = MsgpackRoute

@app.post("/")
async def root(body : dict):
    return body


client = TestClient(app)

input = {"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": "c"}
response = client.post("/", json=input, headers={"Accept": "application/x-msgpack"})
msgpack_out_response = response.content
print(msgpack_out_response)
# b'\x82\xa1a\x93\x01\x02\x03\xa1b\xa1c'
assert msgpack.loads(msgpack_out_response) == input


input = {"a": [1, 2, 3], "b": "c"}
response = client.post("/", json=input, headers={"Accept": "application/json"})
json_out_response = response.content
print(json_out_response)
# b'{"a":[1,2,3],"b":"c"}'

assert json.loads(json_out_response) == input
```

title: route_class not preserved when calling APIRouter.include_router
body:
**Describe the bug**

When calling `APIRouter.include_router`, the class of the included routes should be preserved. Right now, this is not the case -- the `route_class` of the parent router is used. This means that the `route_class` is lost on inclusion if one isn't careful. Worse, it means that even if you manually create a router with multiple route-types present, there is no way to include that router in another without changing the route type of some of the routes.

See the `include_typed_router` function from #521 for an example of how this currently requires a workaround.

I have addressed this in the short PR #538, but wanted to create an issue for discussion in case there is any reason to preserve the current behavior.
author: dmontagu

comments.author: dmontagu

comments.body:
@dmontagu your PR on the deep copy was having trouble on 3.6. Also - do you believe from a performance perspective deepcopy is less performant than reinstantiation ?

@tiangolo ?

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@sandys I noticed the problem with 3.6 but didn’t want to invest more in fixing it without some more feedback. (I’m not sure the changes are likely to be accepted yet even if I did work through the 3.6 issues.)

It’s not about performance — this happens once during app initialization, and will be insignificant from a performance perspective.

The thing that I find awkward about the code as it exists now is that the route is reinstantiated when calling include_router, potentially using a different class than the one that was used to create it in the first place. From a design perspective this feels a little awkward to me — the idea of “including” the router really seems to me more about the path (and possibly the extra dependencies and openapi spec considerations like tags). I feel like the class of the router (and any associated custom initialization of the route) should ideally not be repeated.

I can see multiple ways in which this design is convenient (doing the refactor wasn’t totally straightforward), but it also makes it a little more awkward to extend in creative ways.

title: Catch exception type of Exception and process body request
body:
**Description**

How can I catch exception type of Exception and process body request?

**Additional context**

My example

@app.exception_handler(Exception)
async def exception_callback(request: requests.Request, exc: Exception):
    request_json = await request.json() # Here i want a body, query_params, headers and path of request
    print(request_json)


@app.post('/create_workflow')
async def create_workflow():
    raise Exception(13)
author: Hedgehogues

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
mmm I'm not sure, but I don't think you can consume body in a middleware (see https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/394)

getting the other parameters you mentioned is possible though (note that the `Request` below is from `starlette` and not `requests` I think you imported the wrong one), but should you need the body that won't happen

I'd ask in Starlette if I were you

```
import pytest
from async_asgi_testclient import TestClient
from fastapi import FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

app = FastAPI()


@app.exception_handler(Exception)
async def exception_callback(request: Request, exc: Exception):
    print(request.query_params)
    print(request.headers)
    print(request.path_params)
    print(exc.args)


@app.post("/create_workflow")
async def create_workflow():
    raise Exception(13)


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_raise_exc():
    async with TestClient(app) as client:
        with pytest.raises(Exception) as pe:
            response = await client.post("/create_workflow")
```

comments.author: Hedgehogues

Yes, I saw it. But this code cannot extract body of request. Example of printing:



> Headers({'host': 'localhost:8000', 'connection': 'keep-alive', 'content-length': '82', 'accept': 'application/json', 'origin': 'http://localhost:8000', 'user-agent': 'Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/75.0.3770.142 Safari/537.36', 'content-type': 'application/json', 'referer': 'http://localhost:8000/docs', 'accept-encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br', 'accept-language': 'en-US,en;q=0.9', 'cookie': 'redirect_to=%252F'})
>
> {}
>
> (123,)

I cannot get body of request

you might want to read https://github.com/encode/starlette/issues/495#issuecomment-494008175 as well and ask on Starlette

title: JSON output reordered by pydantic @validator
body:
This isn't a big problem, but I'm wondering if it's possible to keep the JSON output key ordering unaffected by the presence of a Pydantic `@validator(...)`? I like consistency. 🤷‍♂️

I have this pydantic model structure:
```
class IngredientBase(BaseIngredientMixin):
    name: str = None
    unit: str = None
    unit_price: decimal.Decimal = None
    flavour_warning: bool = None

    @validator('unit')
    def valid_units_only(cls, v):
        choices = set(SingleIngredientTable.unit_choices.keys())
        if v and v not in choices:
            raise ValueError(f'must be one of {choices}')
        return v


class IngredientIn(IngredientBase):
    pass


class IngredientNew(IngredientIn):
    name: str = ...
    unit: str = ...
    unit_price: decimal.Decimal = ...


class IngredientOut(IngredientBase, TestModelMixin, TimestampModelMixin, IDModelMixin):
    pass
```

This will produce:
```
{
  "unit": "IU",
  "id": "sing_qOYSyqZhfOcJKHsfVz7tnfP6M",
  "created": 1571357369,
  "updated": 1571418480,
  "is_test": false,
  "name": "Vitamin C",
  "unit_price": 10.568536363534536,
  "flavour_warning": false
}
```
Without the validator, it produces what I would expect:
```
{
  "id": "sing_qOYSyqZhfOcJKHsfVz7tnfP6M",
  "created": 1571357369,
  "updated": 1571418480,
  "is_test": false,
  "name": "Vitamin C",
  "unit": "IU",
  "unit_price": 10.568536363534536,
  "flavour_warning": false
}
```

How can I continue to use `@validator`s and maintain the expected JSON key ordering?

**Edit**: I can pull out the validator into a 'mixin' class that I apply ONLY to the `IngredientIn` and `IngredientNew` models (leaving `IngredientOut` untouched). But is that my only path here?
author: jaddison

comments.author: jaddison
comments.body: @samuelcolvin, @dmontagu mentioned that I should pose this question to you? Might I ask you to weigh in? Thank you!

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body:
I don't see how validators could effect output order, but I could be wrong.

Could you create a minimal example of the problem and ask on pydantic?

There are numerous fields referenced here that aren't in the example, and there's a complicated inheritance landscape that makes working out what's happening very complicated.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@samuelcolvin if you look in `BaseModel._iter`, it is iterating over `self.__dict__`, which I believe is built up incrementally during the validation process. I thought having a `pre=True` validator could result in the key getting set earlier than it might otherwise, and then since dicts get iterated over in the order keys were added, that would explain the ordering change.

The example here doesn’t use `pre=True` but I figured something related might be going on.

comments.author: samuelcolvin
comments.body: Maybe I'm being dumb, but I don't think validators effect the order of `__fields__`.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
@samuelcolvin Yep, looking through the code I don't see an obvious way this could happen. Thanks for checking.

@jaddison If you can produce a (small) self-contained reproducible example, I can look into this more.

comments.author: jaddison
comments.body:
@dmontagu @samuelcolvin - thank you for looking into this.

It isn't (directly) pydantic-related - I replicated my model structure separately and could not reproduce. However...

Instead, **it is related to the `response_model` parameter**; it must force a different method of validation?

To be specific:
* if I remove the `response_model` parameter from the endpoint and return just an instance of that same pydantic model from the endpoint, the key ordering is as I would expect
* reintroducing the `response_model` param manifests the key mis-ordering

**EDIT**: To be clear, you still need to have the validator. However, the model inheritance is not a factor - I believe you can have a single model (no subclassing) with a field validator, and it ought to exhibit this behaviour still (I think)

comments.author: jaddison
comments.body:
Tiny sample, demonstrating the issue:
```
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, validator

app = FastAPI()

class ValidatedModel(BaseModel):
    one: str = None
    two: str = None
    three: str = None

    @validator('two')
    def validate_two(cls, v):
        if v is '1':
            raise ValueError
        return v

# @app.get("/")
@app.get("/", response_model=ValidatedModel)
async def get():
    return {
        "one": "one",
        "two": "two",
        "three": "three"
    }
```

Note that I am on Python 3.7.4.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Confirmed the issue, and that the problem doesn't occur for `pydantic.json()` calls. It looks like this is related to the creation of the secured cloned model; if you drop the `response_model` argument to the decorator the order comes out the same with validator or without:

```python
@app.get("/", response_model=None)
async def get():
    return ValidatedModel(**{
        "one": "one",
        "two": "two",
        "three": "three"
    })
```

I think this will be addressed by https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/pull/812 (which I'll finish up once pydantic v1 is released) and we can refactor fastapi to make use of that functionality. (That change should also result in a >=2x speedup of serialization when specifying a `response_model`, assuming you return a valid BaseModel instance.)

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Yep, I found the problem -- it is in `fastapi.utils`:

```python
def create_cloned_field(field: Field) -> Field:
    original_type = field.type_
    if is_dataclass(original_type) and hasattr(original_type, "__pydantic_model__"):
        original_type = original_type.__pydantic_model__  # type: ignore
    use_type = original_type
    if lenient_issubclass(original_type, BaseModel):
        original_type = cast(Type[BaseModel], original_type)
        use_type = create_model(
            original_type.__name__,
            __config__=original_type.__config__,
            __validators__=original_type.__validators__,  # type: ignore
        )
        for f in original_type.__fields__.values():
            use_type.__fields__[f.name] = f
    ...
```
It passes the validator fields first, then tacks on the non-validator fields. Actually, it overwrites them all, but because the `use_type.__fields__` already has the validated fields as keys, they remain at the start of dictionary (because of how dictionaries are ordered in python 3.6+).

The PR I linked above should help do away with the cloned field, which would resolve this issue.

@jaddison It should be a relatively simple fix to get the field ordering right, and you could create a PR doing that if you really cared; that could probably be merged more quickly.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @jaddison , @dmontagu , and @samuelcolvin for the discussion and debugging here!

I'm looking forward to the much better implementation from Pydantic itself in @dmontagu's PR :tada: :rocket:

title: Async class method dependency raises a ValueError
body:
**Describe the bug**
If you use an async class method as a dependency, a `ValueError` is thrown. It doesn't happen for a non-async method.

Complete error: `ValueError: [KeyError(<class 'coroutine'>), TypeError("'coroutine' object is not iterable"), TypeError('vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute')]` (at `fastapi/encoders.py:106`)

**To Reproduce**

```py
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request

class DependencyClass:
    async def async_dep(self, request: Request):
        return True

    def sync_dep(self, request: Request):
        return True

app = FastAPI()
dependency = DependencyClass()

# Error
@app.get('/async-dep')
def async_dep(r=Depends(dependency.async_dep)):
    return r

# Everything is fine
@app.get('/sync-dep')
def sync_dep(r=Depends(dependency.sync_dep)):
    return r
```

**Expected behavior**
The async class method dependency should be called and its return value injected.

**Environment:**
 - OS: macOS
 - FastAPI Version: 0.42.0
- Python version: 3.7.2

**Additional context**
I believe the issue comes from here:

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/65536cbf63318d111bf608960378d651b6c1596a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L353-L359

Indeed, `inspect.isfunction(call)` will return `False` in case of a class method. Hence, it is [sent to `run_in_threadpool`](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/65536cbf63318d111bf608960378d651b6c1596a/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L453-L456), which never awaits the coroutine, and we end up trying to serialize it instead of its result (hence the `ValueError`).

Changing the check by:

```py
if inspect.isfunction(call) or inspect.ismethod(call):
```

solves the issue. I can make a PR with the fix and unit tests if it helps.
author: frankie567

comments.author: euri10
comments.body:
not sure what the "right" behavior should be but in your `async_dep` (wich itself is not async) you return a coroutine r without awaiting it so it's kind of expected that it doesn't return it's value, is it ?

somehting like this "works" even if I fail to see the use case
```
import asyncio

import uvicorn
from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI
from starlette.requests import Request


class DependencyClass:
    async def async_dep(self, request: Request):
        await asyncio.sleep(0)
        return False

    def sync_dep(self, request: Request):
        return True


app = FastAPI()
dependency = DependencyClass()

# Error
@app.get('/async-dep')
async def authenticate(r=Depends(dependency.async_dep)):
    s = await r
    return s

# Everything is fine
@app.get('/sync-dep')
def authenticate(r=Depends(dependency.sync_dep)):
    return r


if __name__ == '__main__':
    uvicorn.run("679_async_dep_class:app", reload= True)
```

comments.author: frankie567
comments.body:
Well yes, for the sake of simplicity in the example, my async method don't do anything async, but still is a coroutine that needs to be awaited.

The use case is inspired from the async class dependencies, like `OAuth2PasswordBearer`:

https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/65536cbf63318d111bf608960378d651b6c1596a/fastapi/security/oauth2.py#L138-L163

`__call__` *is* async here and, when you inject this dependency, we do get `param`, we don't need to `await` it in the controller function.

So, I think we should be able to do something similar with class methods. For example:

```py
class Authentication:
    def __init__(self, params):
        self.params = params

    async def get_user(self, request: Request) -> User:
        return await self._authenticate(request)

    async def get_active_user(self, request: Request) -> User:
        user = await self._authenticate(request)
        if not user.is_active:
            raise HTTPException()
        return user

    async def _authenticate(self, request: Request) -> User:
        # Do some authentication logic
        return user

authentication = Authentication(params)

@app.get('/active-user')
def active_user(user=Depends(authentication.get_active_user)):
    return user
```

This is useful to share common logic between all the dependencies while providing some specialized behaviour.

IMO, this is clearly a bug as it works flawlessly with async `__call__` and non-async methods.

comments.author: euri10
comments.body: makes lot of sense indeed, was stuck on your version before edit

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: This makes sense to me; @frankie567 I think it's worth a PR.

comments.author: frankie567
comments.body: The fix is merged and released in [0.44.0](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/releases/tag/0.44.0). Let's close it 👍Thanks @tiangolo!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the help here everyone! :clap: :bow: 

Thanks for reporting back and closing the issue @frankie567  :+1: And thanks for the PR/fix! :tada:

title: asyncpg uuid implementation not recognized by fastapi because of too strict typechecking
body:
### Describe the bug

An alternative implementation of the `UUID` type is not serialized by fastapi but throws an error instead. This is because it is of type `asyncpg.pgproto.pgproto.UUID` instead of type `uuid.UUID`. However it is implemented in such a way that it does the exact same things as the regular UUID but just faster. Also,
```python
isinstance(asyncpg.pgproto.pgproto.UUID(), uuid.UUID) == True
```
this should make fastapi able to recognize it as a uuid.

### To Reproduce
1. Create a file called uuid_error.py with:

```Python
import uuid

from fastapi import FastAPI
from asyncpg.pgproto import pgproto

app = FastAPI()


@app.get("/normal_uuid")
def return_normal_uuid():
    regular_uuid = uuid.UUID("a10ff360-3b1e-4984-a26f-d3ab460bdb51")
    assert isinstance(regular_uuid, uuid.UUID)
    return {"normal_uuid": regular_uuid}


@app.get("/fast_uuid")
def return_fast_uuid():
    asyncpg_uuid = pgproto.UUID("a10ff360-3b1e-4984-a26f-d3ab460bdb51")
    assert isinstance(asyncpg_uuid, uuid.UUID)
    return {"fast_uuid": asyncpg_uuid}
```
2. Run it with uvicorn uuid_error:app
3. Open the browser and call the endpoint `/fast_uuid`.
4. It returns 'internal server error' .
5. But I expected it to return `{"fast_uuid":"a10ff360-3b1e-4984-a26f-d3ab460bdb51"}`.

### Expected behavior

I expected fastapi to jsonify the uuid and send, but instead it returns 'internal server error'. The precise error being thrown in the server is:
```
ValueError: [KeyError(<class 'asyncpg.pgproto.pgproto.UUID'>), TypeError("'asyncpg.pgproto.pgproto.UUID' object is not iterable"), TypeError('vars() argument must have __dict__ attribute')]
```

### Environment

- OS: Linux Mint 19 Tara
- FastAPI Version: 0.44.0
- Python version: 3.8.0

### Additional context

I initially made an issue in the asyncpg repo asking them if they could fix it: https://github.com/MagicStack/asyncpg/issues/512 I think it's usefull as additional context for this issue.
The problem can be solved here in the fastapi code by improving the code a little. I'm working on a PR, this line seems to be the issue:
https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/c200bc2240d48a4a27e96fba536351038eafc44f/fastapi/encoders.py#L113
I think it shouldn't use `type()` but it should instead use `isinstance()` this will make for slightly looser typechecking but I think that might be a good thing in this case?
author: RmStorm

comments.author: 1st1
comments.body:
Including an explicit

```python
if isinstance(obj, uuid.UUID):
    return str(obj)
    # or, altrenatively, `return ENCODERS_BY_TYPE[uuid.UUID](obj)`
```

before line https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/c200bc2240d48a4a27e96fba536351038eafc44f/fastapi/encoders.py#L108 would fix this issue.

comments.author: RmStorm
comments.body:
> Including an explicit
> 
> ```python
> if isinstance(obj, uuid.UUID):
>     return str(obj)
>     # or, altrenatively, `return ENCODERS_BY_TYPE[uuid.UUID](obj)`
> ```
> 
> before line
> 
> https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/c200bc2240d48a4a27e96fba536351038eafc44f/fastapi/encoders.py#L108
> would fix this issue.

I think I solved it in a more general way in #756 such that any other objects that subclass custom classes will also work from now one. I think that's nicer than only handling my case.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @RmStorm for the report and PR!

And thanks @1st1 for your tip, an honor to have you here :smile:

title: fastapi function check too strict
body:
I want to use fastapi with callables which return False on a `inspect.is_function` check.

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI
import toolz

def some_function():
    ...

def error_handler():
    ...

endpoint = toolz.excepts(Exception, some_function, error_handler)
app = FastAPI()
app.get("/")(endpoint)

```

Result:

> AssertionError: An endpoint must be a function or method
author: uriva

comments.author: prostomarkeloff
comments.body:
Interesting situation. But I want suggest to you a more clear way for exceptions handling. 
When you are trying to handle almost **all** exceptions (by `Exception`) you will handle any exceptions and it's wrong way. But if you really want handle any exceptions in your application, you may try [use it](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/handling-errors/). But if you really want to do it by your way - try it:
```py
@app.get("/")
async def func("Place here your args and kwargs"):
    return your_callable(*args, **kwargs)
```

comments.author: prostomarkeloff
comments.body: However, i think we should create PR that will add possibility of adding any callable objects.

comments.author: uriva
comments.body: Yeah it's just an example, `functools.partial` and I guess `toolz.compose` don't work either.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Yeah. This makes sense. I was going to document a working example, etc. But it was faster to fix it :man_shrugging: :tada: 

This should be fixed by #977

title: Return value (nested model) not filtered according to response_model
body:
### Describe the bug

I have a `Response` wrapper for my responses that only has a `data` field which contains the actual response data.
This actual data is not filtered according to the specified `response_model` unless I explicitly convert it into the desired response_model.

### To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior with a minimum self-contained file.

Replace each part with your own scenario:

1. Create a file with:

```Python
from typing import TypeVar, Generic, Optional, List

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
from pydantic.generics import GenericModel
from requests import Response
from starlette import status
from starlette.testclient import TestClient

DataT = TypeVar('DataT')


class R(GenericModel, Generic[DataT]):
    """Wrapper for responses"""
    data: Optional[DataT]


class ProjectOut(BaseModel):
    id: int


class ProjectInDB(ProjectOut):
    user_id: int


app = FastAPI()


@app.get(
    '/simple',
    response_model=ProjectOut
)
def get_simple() -> ProjectOut:
    project = ProjectInDB(id=1, user_id=2)

    return project


@app.get(
    '/nested_explicit',
    response_model=R[ProjectOut]
)
def get_nested() -> R[ProjectOut]:
    project = ProjectInDB(id=1, user_id=2)

    return R[ProjectOut](data=ProjectOut(**project.dict()))


@app.get(
    '/nested',
    response_model=R[ProjectOut]
)
def get_nested() -> R[ProjectOut]:
    project = ProjectInDB(id=1, user_id=2)

    return R[ProjectOut](data=project)


def test_response_model():
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response: Response = client.get('/simple')

    assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK

    data: dict = response.json()
    assert 'user_id' not in data.keys()


def test_nested_response_model_explicit():
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response: Response = client.get('/nested_explicit')

    assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK

    response_wrapper: dict = response.json()
    data = response_wrapper['data']
    assert 'user_id' not in data.keys()


def test_nested_response_model():
    with TestClient(app) as client:
        response: Response = client.get('/nested')

    assert response.status_code == status.HTTP_200_OK

    response_wrapper: dict = response.json()
    data = response_wrapper['data']
    assert 'user_id' not in data.keys()
```

3. Run the tests with pytest: the test `test_nested_response_model` fails

### Expected behavior

When returning data, it is filtered according to the specified `response_model`.

### Screenshots

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- FastAPI Version: 0.45.0
- Python version: 3.7.5

### Additional context

Might this be related to https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/675?
author: chbndrhnns

comments.author: chbndrhnns
comments.body:
I also tried changing my wrapper class to this and it still fails:

```python
class R(GenericModel, Generic[DataT]):
    """Wrapper for responses"""
    data: Optional[ProjectOut]
```

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Well this is an interesting bug; it actually passes for me on older versions of FastAPI/pydantic so I think it might be an issue with the 1.0 upgrade.

Thank you for putting together an easily-reproduced test case, I'll see if I can figure it out.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I found the issue: it's because `dict(model)` now no longer does a deep conversion of a model into its dict, only the top layer, and this is used by `BaseModel.validate`.

I see various possible fixes:
1) Change `BaseModel.validate` in pydantic to use `value_as_dict = value.dict()` instead of `value_as_dict = dict(value)`. I think this is non-preferable since the current approach is faster and may be preferable in a non-FastAPI context.
2) Change FastAPI to perform the dict conversion itself. There are various potential ways one could do this, but most would probably involve substantial overhead for dumping and reparsing (maybe that's similar to what's happening now anyway though). For example, adding `response = jsonable_encoder(response)` would solve this completely, though would likely be somewhat slower than the (old, working version of the) current approach.
3) Fix the secure cloned field creation: (it seems like it's not recursing down to submodels and cloning them as well; that didn't matter before because pydantic recursively converted the whole object to dict-ified form).
4) Implement `dump_as_type` in pydantic, and use it to get rid of the reparsing and secured cloned field entirely. This is my strong preference, since it will have very large performance benefits beyond fix this bug, and would provide a means for other frameworks to much more simply leverage similar funcitonality, but it presents a substantial addition to pydantic and may take some time/effort. I opened a PR for this a while ago but it was a little too large in scope; I've added some of the other pieces to pydantic since then so now could be a good time to add the rest.


Also, in case there is any confusion, this issue is not specific to generics -- it fails in the same way if you replace `R[ProjectOut]` with `RProjectOut` for
```python
class RProjectOut(BaseModel):
    """Wrapper for responses"""
    data: Optional[ProjectOut]
```

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @chbndrhnns for the well-written report and test! :bug: 

And thanks @dmontagu as always :rocket: 

It should be fixed by #889, just released in the latest version `0.47.1`.

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->

title: remove ujson dependency (it is dead)
body:
FastAPI is using `ujson` which has not been actively developed for about 3 years by now. [Here is a relevant issue](https://github.com/esnme/ultrajson/issues/343). It's starting to be problematic to actually compile `ujson` on newer platforms.

Possible alternative maybe `orjson`.
author: hnykda

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I would be in favor of recommending `orjson` instead of `ujson`.

I recall the creator of `orjson` reaching about this many months ago and there was some confusion due to slow responses, and also some issues on the pydantic side, but I think many/most of the barriers to usage have since been removed. If `orjson` seems to be taking over as the go-to high-performance json library, I think it would make sense to use that for development and recommend it.

To the extent that there is any difference in json encoding/decoding between json and orjson (or ujson and orjson), it would probably be best to document it.

I'd be willing to review a PR making this change (including updates to the docs). (I'd leave the ultimate decision about merging to @tiangolo though.)

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Yeah, I've been intending to do it for quite some time now... :sweat_smile: 

In fact, you can sort of do it right now (and since several versions ago). 

---

Here's my plan:

I want to export everything from Starlette, to avoid confusions of the type "do I import this from FastAPI or from Starlette?", I've seen reports of that and even I've struggled with that. For example, FastAPI has its own `HTTPException`.

With that, I want to have a response `ORJSONResponse` exported from `fastapi.responses`, among Starlette's responses.

In fact, I added tests for creating custom responses (as part of a PR), testing specifically creating orjson responses: https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/f803c77515662cd2382674d9fc8df1e6641b3ba7/tests/test_default_response_class.py#L9-L13
And you can replicate creating that response class and using it. It's just 3 lines (you would remove the custom `media_type`), but still, it should be provided by FastAPI (among all the other responses from Starlette).

```Python
app = FastAPI(default_response_class=ORJSONResponse)
```

---

Also, as a side note, UJSON is not really used by default anywhere. It's optional, and you would have to use the `UJSONResponse` class for it to be used, the same way you would use the (new/custom/future) `ORJSONResponse`.

comments.author: hnykda
comments.body:
Well, that's elegant sir :bowing_man: :clap: .

> Also, as a side note, UJSON is not really used by default anywhere. It's optional, and you would have to use the UJSONResponse class for it to be used, the same way you would use the (new/custom/future) ORJSONResponse.

Then I don't understand why it's in [requirements](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/master/pyproject.toml#L81). It's [written](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi#optional-dependencies) that it's an optional dependency for Pydantic, but it seems that Pydantic has already moved away from ujson [here](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/issues/589). Seems that their [requirements](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/master/requirements.txt) nor [setup.py](https://github.com/samuelcolvin/pydantic/blob/master/setup.py) don't have it anymore.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: @hnykda the “all” set of requirements there includes everything necessary to make use of *all documented features*, including those that are optional. It is intended more for development/reference purposes than for use by consumers.

comments.author: hnykda
comments.body:
Ah, got it! Thanks. I believe this can be closed then.

Personally, I would not reference any obsolete dependencies in my project even if they are optional (especially "future-looking" like Python 3.6+, latest libs, ...) . Reasons for that: helping the community to get rid dangerous/old/unmaintained libs and to avoid possibly repealing people who do not like to install stuff which are referencing such unmaintained projects.

comments.author: dagnelies
comments.body:
Apparently, `ujson` found a new maintainer and is now alive and kicking again. Well, dunno about the kicking part, but releases are pretty fresh: 

https://pypi.org/project/ujson/

and repo seems active again. Just mentioning this. Dunno which one is the better lib.

comments.author: dagnelies
comments.body: Nevermind, apparently `orjson` is a fine choice too. Apparently one of the fastest.

comments.author: ahmadazizi
comments.body: Just to mention, installing fastapi on windows platform (python 3.9) fails, since orjson neither provide the compatible wheel nor could be compiled.

comments.author: jacopofar
comments.body:
[On Debian stable installing orjson currently fails](https://github.com/ijl/orjson/issues/136) because it targets `manylinux2014` which is supported by pip since version `19.3`, but Debian stable install pip `18.1` (if installed through Debian repos `python3-pip`).

In this case running `python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip` works, but the error can be tricky to detect

comments.author: ahtik
comments.body: @ahmadazizi,  `orjson` wheels are now available also for Windows Python 3.9.

comments.author: ycd
comments.body:
Since Starlette dropped support for `UJSONResponse` in https://github.com/encode/starlette/pull/1047 (Requires Starlette 0.14.1), we can remove it too, `ujson` causes build fails on many distributions due C Dependencies (See #2811 ). 

This require changes in #2335, since it re-adds `UJSONResponse`.

comments.author: omidmaldar
comments.body: Regarding ujson, there is a [security alert](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-fh56-85cw-5pq6) reported with moderate severity which is patched in 5.1.0.  Fastapi 0.74.0 [requires](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/1ce16c2f40a75a4d97233bf4e331ed563eda47cf/pyproject.toml#L93) `ujson (>=4.0.1,<5.0.0)` which is not safe.

comments.author: melt7777
comments.body:
Hello all, I did find a workaround which allows us to build images without the ujson. I am using the `python-poetry` package manager, instead of requirements.txt. 

FastAPI is fantastic so far, thank you all for your hard work on this project. 

Here, roughly the steps I used:

1. `poetry init` 
2. `poetry add fastapi -E all` - it installed the ujson package and placed the ujson bits into the pyproject.toml and the newly created poetry.lock file. 
3. Manually edit the .toml and .lock files and remove all the ujson items.
4. Build image as usual and it will not have the ujson or its vuln in the image. 

Hope this helps someone until they scan make the permanent fix.

Thank you,

~ melt

comments.author: alexted
comments.body:
Are you sure the **ujson** is EOL? According to [its repository](https://github.com/ultrajson/ultrajson), the last release was in mid-September, and the last commit was 3 weeks ago - the project seems to be quite alive.
Another point is that it is inferior to **orjson** in performance. In that case, it can be left as an option.

title: Support repeated key=value in form data
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem

Yes.

Given some URL encoded data like this...

```
choices=parrot&choices=spider
```

...only the last key=value wins.

This does not work like I expected:
```python
choices: list = Form(...)
```

You can only validate against the last value.

### The solution you would like

Perhaps FastAPI should collect repeated keys in the 2-tuple list that `request.form()` gives and assign those values as a list to the same key before validation happens.
author: ghost

comments.author: shifr
comments.body:
Hey @StephenCarboni, parameters in the URL are part of a querystring and you can get and validate them as described here: https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/query-params-str-validations/#query-parameter-list-multiple-values
As far as I understand, `Form()` is using only for request body

comments.author: shifr
comments.body: Oh, sorry, looks like I misread you message.

comments.author: shifr
comments.body: Anyway, I think this issue better be created in the starlette repo, because the `request` is an object from starlette.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
I am a little surprised that `choices=parrot&choices=spider` doesn't work; if I recall correctly it definitely works for query parameters, and is how you would send a list of values.

The logic for form data parsing is in the `starlette.requests.Request.form` method, and delegates to [`python-multipart`](https://github.com/andrew-d/python-multipart), so you can look for the specific logic there.

(Since it uses an entirely different library for parsing, it ultimately isn't THAT surprising that the logic might be different.)

comments.author: nsidnev
comments.body:
@dmontagu 
Hi! I found some strange behavior regarding this issue. `FastAPI` can correctly validate the list from the form if the parameter is declared as `typing.List[str]`. But if I declare it with `list` annotation, then I get a validation error that the passed data is not a list. I don’t think this is a `Starlette` issue, because calling the `request.form()` returns the correct results that can be obtained by calling `FormData.getlist("choices")`.

Here is my example application:
```python3
from typing import List

from fastapi import FastAPI, Form
from starlette.requests import Request

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/typing-list-annotation")
async def typing_list(choices: List[str] = Form(...)) -> dict:
    return {"choices": choices}


@app.post("/simple-list-annotation")
async def simple_list(choices: list = Form(...)) -> dict:
    return {"choices": choices}


@app.post("/request-passing")
async def request_passing(request: Request) -> dict:
    return {"choices": (await request.form()).getlist("choices")}

```

And example of wrong request:
```bash
$ http -f -v POST :8000/simple-list-annotation choices=a choices=2               
POST /simple-list-annotation HTTP/1.1
Accept: */*
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 19
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded; charset=utf-8
Host: localhost:8000
User-Agent: HTTPie/1.0.3

choices=a&choices=2

HTTP/1.1 422 Unprocessable Entity
content-length: 98
content-type: application/json
date: Tue, 14 Jan 2020 02:31:04 GMT
server: uvicorn

{
    "detail": [
        {
            "loc": [
                "body",
                "choices"
            ],
            "msg": "value is not a valid list",
            "type": "type_error.list"
        }
    ]
}
```

The problem is in [this](https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/blob/3eca945bd17600a0be6eae1d8fd93ba8f9805006/fastapi/dependencies/utils.py#L632) line. When a form parameter is converted to a `pydantic` field, it is identified as `pydantic.fields.SHAPE_SINGLETON` instead of `pydantic.fields.SHAPE_LIST` when the `list` annotation is used. After that, when validating the form, `FastAPI` is unable to understand that this is a list and tries to get a sinlge value which causes `ValidationError`. I've pushed a PR that should fix this.

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body: Yep, this seems to be an awkwardness with pydantic; thanks for submitting the fix!

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @StephenCarboni for the report :mag: :male_detective: 

Thanks @nsidnev for the fix! :tada: :rocket: 

And thanks everyone here for the discussion.

The fix is available in the latest release.

comments.author: jhaoheng
comments.body:
Hi, 

```
@app.post("/root")
async def root(ids: List[str] = Form(...)):
    return ids
```

- fastapi version : 0.61.0

I use CURL to run the api, it works ...
But, if I use `/docs` (the default UI) to run this api, return `["obj_1, obj_2"]`
I think it should be `["obj_1", "obj_2"]`

comments.author: HitLuca
comments.body:
I think we still have an issue with `Form` and `List`. When passing a list of elements as a `Form` field, it doesn't get parsed correctly:

```python
@app.put("/test1")
def test1(values: List[str] = Form(...)) -> None:
    return values


@app.put("/test2")
def test2(values: List[str] = Query(...)) -> None:

```

test1 example

```bash
curl -X 'PUT' \
  'http://localhost:5050/test1' \
  -H 'accept: application/json' \

```

]
```

curl -X 'PUT' \

  -H 'accept: application/json'
```

we can see how `Query` correctly parses the list, whereas `Form` doesn't. Changing `List[str]` to `list` doesn't fix the problem. Accepting list of numbers with `List[int]` for example returns an error as shown below, indicating that `typing.List` isn't handled as expected

```python
@app.put("/test3")
def test3(values: List[int] = Form(...)) -> None:
    print(type(values), values)
    return values
```

test3 example

```bash

```

      ],

    }

}
```

title: Nested pydantic model in response model doesn't filter additional data
body:
### Describe the bug

In [Response Model documentation](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/response-model/), there is an example for `FastAPI will take care of filtering out all the data that is not declared in the output model`. However, this doesn't happen for a nested model.

### To Reproduce

1. Create a file with:

```Python
from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class ModelB(BaseModel):
    username: str


class ModelC(ModelB):
    password: str


class ModelA(BaseModel):
    name: str
    description: str = None
    model_b: ModelB


async def get_model_c() -> ModelC:
    return ModelC(username="test-user", password="test-password")


@app.get("/model", response_model=ModelA)
async def get_model_a(model_c=Depends(get_model_c)):
    return {"name": "model-a-name", "description": "model-a-desc", "model_b": model_c}
```

3. Open the browser and call the endpoint `/model`.
4. It returns a JSON with `{"name":"model-a-name","description":"model-a-desc","model_b":{"username":"test-user","password":"test-password"}}`.
5. But I expected it to return `{"name":"model-a-name","description":"model-a-desc","model_b":{"username":"test-user"}}`.

### Expected behavior

Filtering should be done for nested model attributes too.

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 18.04.3 LTS
- FastAPI 0.45.0
- Python 3.8.1
author: sagrawal-idrc

comments.author: chbndrhnns
comments.body: I see this related to https://github.com/tiangolo/fastapi/issues/800

comments.author: dmontagu
comments.body:
Yep, this really needs to be fixed. I haven't had much free time lately but I'll try to get around to fixing this soon.

As a short term workaround, it should work properly if you call `return jsonable_encoder(<current response>)` where jsonable_encoder is `fastapi.encoders.jsonable_encoder`. But obviously it would be better if this wasn't required.

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks for the easy to replicate and test report @sagrawal-idrc ! :bug: 

And thanks @dmontagu for the help here :muscle: 

And yeah, @chbndrhnns , it was the same issue.

It should be fixed by #889, just released in the latest version `0.47.1`.

<!-- issue-manager: answered -->

title: Support request body without pydantic
body:
Thank you very much for developing and maintaining FastAPI, it's great 🙂 

### Is your feature request related to a problem

I tried to create a POST route with `username: str` and `password: str` parameters in the function signature. I was expecting FastAPI to search for `username` and `password` in the request body (since I used `@app.post`), but instead it gets them from the URL, as query parameters. It seems it only works when the parameter is a Pydantic model, not a builtin type.

### The solution you would like

With 
```python
@app.post("/route/")
def my_route(username: str, password: str):
    ...
```

Accept `username` and `password` as fields of the request body, not as query parameters.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Use Pydantic models. It works fine, it's just a bit less straight-forward.

```python
class LoginForm(BaseModel):
    username: str
    password: str

@app.post("/route/")
def my_route(login_data: LoginForm):
    ...
```

It could also be emphasized in the docs (https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/body/) that it will not work with built-in types, only with Pydantic models.

### Additional context

I'm not sure my feature request makes sense: generally speaking, if POST requests can also use query parameters in the URL, then it's impossible to distinguish the ones that should be in the request body and the ones that appear in the URL as query parameters. It means it would not be possible to build the openapi.json I guess. In that case, please close this as invalid 🙂
author: pawamoy

comments.author: nsidnev
comments.body:
@pawamoy 
Hi! I think you are looking for [this part](https://fastapi.tiangolo.com/tutorial/body-multiple-params/#singular-values-in-body) of the documentation. Is that what you need?

So your example will look something like this:
```python3
from fastapi import FastAPI, Body

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/route/")
def my_route(username: str = Body(...), password: str = Body(...)) -> None:
    print("username:", username)
    print("password length:", len(password))
```

comments.author: pawamoy
comments.body: Indeed! Thank you for pointing me to this page. This feature request can definitely be closed. I'm really sorry for the noise! Cheers

comments.author: tiangolo
comments.body:
Thanks @nsidnev for your help here! :nerd_face: :cake: 

Thanks @pawamoy for reporting back and closing the issue :taco: :rocket:

title: app.frontend() with fallback="index.html" returns 404 for client side routes that have a dot in the last path segment (like /users/john.doe or /v/1.2.3)
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/fastapi/fastapi/discussions/15979
Originally posted by **UditDewan** July 12, 2026 ### First Check - [x] I added a very descriptive title here. - [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it. - [x] I searched the FastAPI documentation, with the integrated search. - [x] I already searched in Google "How to X in FastAPI" and didn't find any information. - [x] I already read and followed all the tutorial in the docs and didn't find an answer. - [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to Pydantic. - [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to Swagger UI. - [x] I already checked if it is not related to FastAPI but to ReDoc. ### Commit to Help - [x] I commit to help with one of those options 👆 ### Example Code ```python import os import tempfile from fastapi import FastAPI from fastapi.testclient import TestClient dist = tempfile.mkdtemp() with open(os.path.join(dist, "index.html"), "w") as f: f.write("SPA shell") app = FastAPI() app.frontend("/", directory=dist, fallback="index.html") client = TestClient(app) # The Accept header a browser sends on a normal top level navigation. browser_nav = { "accept": "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9," "image/avif,image/webp,image/apng,*/*;q=0.8" } print(client.get("/users/john", headers=browser_nav).status_code) # 200, serves index.html print(client.get("/users/john.doe", headers=browser_nav).status_code) # 404 print(client.get("/v/1.2.3", headers=browser_nav).status_code) # 404 ``` ### Description Hi 👋 I have been trying out the new `app.frontend()` helper to serve a single page app, using `fallback="index.html"` so that client side routes fall back to the shell and the frontend router can take over. This is the behavior described in the "Client side routing" part of the docs, and it works fine for a path like `/dashboard` or `/dashboard/settings`. The problem shows up when a client side route has a dot in its last path segment. Plenty of real apps have these, for example a profile at `/users/john.doe`, a version deep link at `/v/1.2.3`, or a slug like `/files/report.2024`. When I open one of those directly, or just hit refresh while I am already on that page, I get a 404 instead of the `index.html` shell, so the app never loads and the client router never gets a chance to handle the route. The part that surprised me is that setting `fallback="index.html"` explicitly does not change this. As far as I can tell there is currently no way to get these routes to fall back to the shell. I spent some time reading the code and I think it comes from `_is_frontend_navigation_request()` in `fastapi/routing.py`. It takes the last path segment and if `os.path.splitext(...)` finds an extension it returns early and treats the request as an asset request, before it ever looks at the `Accept` header. Because `os.path.splitext("john.doe")` returns `.doe`, any segment with a dot in it is treated as a file and never gets the `index.html` fallback, even when the browser clearly asked for `text/html`. What makes me think this is fixable without regressions is that the two situations are easy to tell apart. A real browser navigation sends `Accept: text/html`, while a missing asset requested from a script tag, an image tag, or a fetch call sends `*/*` or a specific type like `text/css`. If I request a genuinely missing asset such as `/assets/missing.js` with `Accept: */*` it still returns 404 as expected, so checking the `Accept` header first would fix the navigation case and keep the asset case working. I did notice there is already a test that pins the current behavior. `test_index_fallback_does_not_handle_asset_like_or_non_html_requests` has a `("/users/jane.doe", "text/html")` case that expects a 404, so before proposing a change I wanted to ask whether the current behavior is intentional. If the idea is that dotted client routes should be servable, would a change that treats an explicit `Accept: text/html` request as navigation regardless of the path be acceptable? Or would you rather keep the extension heuristic as the default for `fallback="auto"` and add an opt in for apps that use dotted client side routes? I am happy to open a PR with a failing test and the change for whichever direction you prefer. Thanks a lot for the work on this feature. ### Operating System Windows ### Operating System Details Also reproduced on current master. The logic involved is pure routing code, so it is not tied to any particular operating system. ### FastAPI Version 0.139.0 ### Pydantic Version 2.13.4 ### Python Version 3.11 ### Additional Context _No response_
